Where are the extension workers?. Home»Agribusiness»Where are the extension workers? by HENRY KINYUA Farmers in Kenya are hungry for information on the best farming practices, how to maximize yields, ensure better crop varieties and anything that will enhance their farms.  But are they getting it? Unfortunately, the agriculture sector as currently constituted in Kenya has very little education flowing to the majority of farmers. It is not surprising that we cannot feed ourselves. As a result, agricultural produce has been flowing from neighboring countries. Recently there have been concerns about fish imports from China. How did we get here? Kenya last employed agriculture extension workers in 2006. Before that, they had been last recruited in 1986. This means that in the next 5 years, over 50% of current extension workers will be retiring. The ratio of extension workers to the farmer is at best 1: 1000 nationally. At county levels, it is as high as 1:2000. This means there is an acute shortage of extension workers and the few available cannot meet the needs of farmers. Agriculture extension is still too poorly funded to reach the essential staff: farmer ratio. Does the recently launched Agriculture Sector Transformation and Growth Strategy address this issue? It is encouraging that under an initiative dubbed Flagship 7, the strategy has plans to “hire 200 county and national transformation leaders, hire 3000 youth Extension workers, train and equip them to deliver digitally enabled agriculture research and extension and finally support 1000 farmer facing SMEs”. This, among other things, is expected to lower extension workers: farmer ratio to 1:600. The strategy notes that there will be established a national office to provide guidelines to counties on how to do this. One hopes suffcient funding to support this will be available. No development in agriculture is possible without a significant investment in extension and research. By Kimuri Mwangi For Kyalo Mutua, the journey into agribusiness financing has come full circle. Aflatoxins: The good, the bad and the business news. Home»Agribusiness»Aflatoxins: The good, the bad and the business news by HENRY KINYUA Ten years ago the late Dr Kioko Mangeli, former Managing Director of the Kenya Bureau of Standards, revealed that some 6,350-tonnes of imported maize was unfit for human consumption due to high levels of Aflatoxins. As a result, he predicted it would lead to an increase of cancer cases in Kenya within a few years. The ongoing cancer crisis has revived interest in Dr Mangeli’s words and the Aflatoxins issue. Aflatoxins are any of a class of toxic compounds  produced by certain fungi (moulds) found in food, which can cause liver  damage and cancer. The fungi responsible for most poisoning is called  Aspergillus flavus. Aflatoxins have been reported in grains and nuts in Kenya from the turn of the century. So it is a problem we have had for a long time. The contaminated imports reported on by Dr. Mangeli therefore added to an existing problem in the country. For instance, in 2004, a major outbreak of aflatoxin poisoning occurred in Kenya. 317 cases were reported resulting in the deaths of 39% of reported cases. This reportedly resulted from consumption of contaminated maize. A study that has sampled flours from shelves of supermarkets in Kenya showed high levels of Aflatoxins. One of my friends even said he will not be eatingugali, although I am yet to follow up on whether or not he has kept his vow. If Aflatoxins have been a problem
for such a long time in Kenya, what has been done about it? This takes us to the good news: There have been a lot of efforts in managing Aflatoxins by various stakeholders, from sensitizing farmers on good agricultural practices and post-handling practices to the latest technology called AFLASAFE; a biological control that enables 4 types of good fungi to grow on maize while outcompeting the bad fungi (poison causing) in maize and nuts. This renders the bad fungi incapable of surviving. It is applied in the farms before tussling/flowering of maize for effective control. Aflasafe was developed through a partnership
between Kenya Agricultural and Livestock Research Organization (KALRO) and the International
Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA). KALRO has set up a manufacturing
plant at their Katumani Facility in Machakos county. They have produced enough stocks
to cover all maize produced in the country. This leads us to the Business News: KALRO have floated a tender inviting proposals for a business opportunity to market and distribute Aflasafe. If you are in that business please visit KALRO’s website (www.kalro.org), download the tender documents and do as requested. Are farmers poisoning us?. Home»Agribusiness»Are farmers poisoning us? by HENRY KINYUA In the last one month, we have witnessed  the deaths of some high-profile Kenyans due to cancer. As a result, the statement “Farmers are killing us with chemicals” has been on the tongues of many people. So, are farmers truly contributing to these deaths? To better understand this let’s delve into the chemicals that farmers apply on crops and the possibility of them indirectly poisoning human beings. Crops, like human beings, get sick and require nutrition. Therefore, farmers use various chemicals, either organic or inorganic, to control diseases, minimize infestation and improve nutrition. Chemicals used in crops are categorized by the organisms they control or nutrients they provide. So, we have insecticides  to control insects, fungicides to control fungal infections, herbicides to control weeds, bactericides (although not common) to control bacterial infections. We add fertilizers to provide nutrients to crops to supplement what is available in the soil or other growing media used. Insecticides and other ‘cides’ are also generally referred to as Pesticides. Pesticides are of different categories or chemical classes and have different levels of toxicity.  Therefore, if they are used incorrectly, they can cause poisoning that can lead to ailments. To prevent this, use of inputs in crop production has to follow the “4 Rs”. That is: theRightchemical, used at theRighttime, in theRightmanner and applied in theRightdoses. For this to happen, the farmer or his/her advisor must identify the pest early enough and establish the right control measure. If they choose chemical intervention, they must then get the right one, which can be an insecticide/fungicide or a bactericide. They must then identify the right time to apply it and choose the right method of application, for example, spraying the leaves. They should then pick the right equipment and measure the right dose. If they don’t do this, it can lead to human poisoning or environmental pollution. Most importantly farmers must observe the Post-Harvest Intervals (PHI). This is the period that farmers must wait after applying chemicals before harvesting. This period is determined by how long it takes for the pesticide molecule to breakdown to levels that cannot cause any human poisoning. Different methods are used to measure this, but the most universally accepted one is the MRLs (Maximum Residue Levels) of a given chemical(s) that are allowed in food products. The European Union (EU) and Japan are very strict about this. Any product exceeding the set MRLs is destroyed. It is important to note that not all chemicals used in crops are carcinogenic (cancer-causing) and consuming crop products that have exceeded MRLs does not necessarily mean that it will cause illness. Additionally, various certification standards are introduced to ensure that the entire production process doesn’t encourage poisoning or pollution to occur at any point. This, as you might have noted, is a very technical process and farmers on their own don’t have the necessary skills to make these decisions. So, before we point our fingers at farmers, let’s look at the extension system in the country and see where the problem lies. Kenya Rolls Out Five-Year KSh1.081 Trillion Agricultural Investment Strategy. Home»Agriculture Policy»Kenya Rolls Out Five-Year KSh1.081 Trillion Agricultural Investment Strategy By Kimuri Mwangi Kenya has unveiled an ambitious Sh1.081 trillion National Agri-Food Systems Investment Plan (NASIP) 2026-2030, setting out a comprehensive roadmap to modernise agriculture, strengthen food security, create more than two million jobs and position the country as a regional hub for sustainable agri-food investment. The investment blueprint was officially launched during the third Financing Agri-Food Systems (FINAS) Summit at the Kenyatta International Convention Centre (KICC) in Nairobi. The plan forms part of the implementation of Kenya’s Agricultural Sector Transformation and Growth Strategy (ASTGS) and aligns with continental commitments under the Comprehensive Africa Agriculture Development Programme (CAADP) and the Kampala Declaration. Presiding over the launch on behalf of Agriculture and Livestock Development Cabinet Secretary Mutahi Kagwe, Principal Secretary for State Department for Livestock Development, Jonathan Mueke, described the investment plan as a major milestone in Kenya’s agricultural transformation agenda. He noted that NASIP is a fully costed investment framework designed to coordinate government priorities, county aspirations, private sector opportunities and development partner support under a single implementation framework. “NASIP is Kenya’s national investment blueprint for transforming our agri-food systems. It provides a clear, credible, and coordinated pipeline of opportunities across crops, livestock, fisheries, irrigation, agro-industrialisation, digital agriculture, climate resilience, research and innovation, as well as agricultural finance,” he said. Valued at Sh1.081 trillion over five years, the investment plan seeks to finance strategic interventions across the country’s agricultural value chains while replacing fragmented projects with coordinated investments tied to measurable outcomes. According to Mueke, financing will be mobilised through a partnership involving national and county governments, the private sector and development partners. “This investment will be mobilised through a strategic partnership in which the Government of Kenya, together with our county governments, will contribute 35 percent. The private sector then is expected to contribute 45 percent, and the development and bilateral partners’ share is 20 percent of the total investment envelope,” he said. The Ministry said the plan aims to build resilient food systems by modernising agricultural value chains, expanding irrigation, improving food security, raising farmers’ incomes and creating over two million jobs, particularly for young people and women. The launch coincided with implementation of the 2026-2035 CAADP Strategy and Action Plan, as African countries begin operationalising commitments made under the Kampala Declaration. Addressing delegates at the summit, Mueke said Africa possesses enormous agricultural potential but continues to face persistent food insecurity and inadequate investment. “Africa is home to nearly 60% of the world’s remaining uncultivated arable land. We have abundant natural resources, a youthful and entrepreneurial population, and immense agricultural potential. Yet, despite these advantages, our continent remains a net importer of food.” He said climate change, volatile global markets and rising food demand have exposed the urgent need for sustainable financing mechanisms capable of transforming Africa’s agri-food systems. “The challenge before us now is mobilising the investment, partnerships, and accountability needed to translate all these commitments into measurable results for our farmers, our businesses, and indeed, our economies.” Mueke argued that governments alone cannot finance agricultural transformation and called for greater private sector participation. “Governments must therefore play a different role, not as a sole financier, but an enabler of investment, a manager of risk, and a trusted partner in creating the conditions for sustainable private capital to flourish.” The Principal Secretary said Kenya has complemented NASIP with the recently launched AgriConnect Compact, which outlines the country’s commitments under the Kampala Declaration. Together, he said, the two instruments provide an integrated investment framework that translates policy commitments into bankable projects capable of attracting domestic and international investment. He added that the plan covers priority investments in crops, livestock, fisheries, irrigation, agro-industrialisation, digital agriculture, climate resilience, agricultural finance, research and innovation. While acknowledging Kenya has developed several agricultural strategies over the years, Mueke said implementation has often been undermined by inadequate financing. “We always come with very good plans, very good policies and strategies on how we are going to increase production. But there’s always a gap that glares, which is financing. How do we finance all these policies, plans, and strategies to ensure that they actually get implemented and executed?” He explained that NASIP introduces a clear financing strategy designed to bridge that gap. The government plans to prioritise value chains identified under the Bottom-Up Economic Transformation Agenda, including tea, rice, edible oils and macadamia in the crop sector, alongside leather, meat and dairy in livestock. He also highlighted President William Ruto’s commitment to increase the capitalisation of the Agricultural Finance Corporation (AFC) from Sh2 billion to Sh10 billion to improve farmers’ access to affordable credit. Mueke further called on commercial banks to expand lending to agriculture, saying the sector should become one of the largest components of banks’ loan portfolios. Held under thetheme “Towards Sustainable Financial Architecture for Africa’s Food Systems,”the summit brought together governments, financial institutions, development agencies, researchers, agribusinesses and policymakers to explore innovative approaches to financing Africa’s food systems. FINAS Summit Director Dr Charity Mutegi said the platform has evolved beyond policy discussions and is now focused on delivering measurable outcomes. “In its third edition, FINAS 2026 has continued to advance sustainable finance as a lever for meaningful change in Africa, laying the ground for the unveiling of a private-sector-led agriculture finance working group.” She said discussions at the summit centred on blended finance, climate-smart investments, innovative agricultural financing solutions and practical country experiences. Mutegi stressed that Africa must rethink how agricultural financing is structured rather than simply increasing funding levels. “Since independence, most African nations cannot continue doing the same thing in terms of the structuring of financing for food systems and expect a different result.” She noted that one of the summit’s major achievements was the official launch of Kenya’s National Agri-Food Systems Investment Plan. “One of the outcomes, and the PS has just talked about it, is the launch of NASIP. Kenya is one of the first countries to actually have a country instrument to respond to CAADP at the continental level.” Mutegi added that the summit is expected to establish a private-sector agriculture finance working group involving the Kenya Bankers Association and other stakeholders to strengthen collaboration with government and improve the policy environment for food systems financing. Several international development partners used the summit to reaffirm support for Africa’s agricultural transformation agenda. Irish Ambassador to Kenya Caitríona Ingoldsby said international partnerships remain essential in transforming Africa’s food systems. “Africa’s food systems transformation requires strong partnerships that bring together governments, development partners, the private sector and smallholder farming communities.” AGRA Vice President for Programme Delivery Prof. Hamadi Idi Boga urged African countries to move beyond commitments and accelerate implementation. “Africa has no shortage of strategies or commitments. What is needed now is implementation at scale.” Germany also called for increased investment in sustainable agriculture. Maren Kneller, Head of Development Cooperation at the German Embassy in Kenya, said governments must continue creating enabling environments that reduce investment risks and attract private capital. Meanwhile, Food Systems for the Future (FSD Kenya) CEO Rashmi Pillai underscored the need for inclusive financing models that place smallholder farmers at the centre of agricultural transformation. “As a sector, we need inclusive finance for sustainable agri-food systems. These systems should work for MSMEs and small-scale farmers to get the financial support which works for them.” Mueke challenged governments, investors, development finance institutions and private sector players to convert dialogue into investment and tangible action. “Africa does not lack opportunity. Africa does not lack innovation. Neither does Africa lack entrepreneurs. What we must unlock as our continent is investment.” He said Kenya is ready to work with investors and development partners to build resilient, competitive and inclusive agri-food systems capable of improving livelihoods, strengthening food security and driving long-term economic growth. Kenya Launches Horticulture Traceability System to Strengthen Export Compliance and Market Access. Home»Agriculture Policy»Kenya Launches Horticulture Traceability System to Strengthen Export Compliance and Market Access By Kimuri Mwangi Kenya has launched a National Horticulture Traceability System (NHTS), a digital platform designed to track horticultural produce from the farm to international markets, in a move aimed at enhancing food safety compliance, boosting confidence among global buyers and protecting one of the country’s leading export sectors. The system, commissioned by the Agriculture and Food Authority (AFA), comes amid increasing demands from export markets for greater transparency on the origin of produce, farming practices and the use of agricultural inputs. Regulators and industry players say the platform will enable authorities to trace produce throughout the supply chain, improve accountability and respond more effectively to food safety concerns. Speaking during the launch, AFA Chairman Cornelly Serem said the system addresses a growing demand from international buyers for detailed information about the crops they import. “Today, we are commissioning the National Horticulture Traceability System. The system that will guide either importers or exporters. We have more importers wanting to know where the crops are coming from and which pesticides are being used, and even the chemicals that are used during planting. In that sense, the system will allow the importer to know even the fertilisers that we are using in planting and tracing of the same,” said Serem. The launch marks a significant milestone for Kenya’s horticulture industry, which remains one of the country’s most important economic sectors. According to AFA, horticulture contributes significantly to the economy, provides employment to more than four million people and supports food and nutrition security, local trade and agro-processing industries. In 2025, Kenya exported 457,860 tonnes of horticultural produce valued at Sh143.7 billion. Flowers accounted for 55 per cent of export earnings, fruits 30 per cent and vegetables 15 per cent. The European Union and the Middle East remain the leading export destinations, while markets in India, China, Eastern Europe, Scandinavia and North Africa continue to expand. Kenya is currently Africa’s leading exporter of flowers and avocados and ranks among the world’s top exporters of both commodities. Last year, flower exports generated Sh81 billion while fresh avocado and avocado oil exports earned Sh25.85 billion. Despite these achievements, the industry continues to face challenges ranging from high production costs and climate change to pests, diseases and increasingly stringent food safety requirements in international markets. According to AFA, one of the major challenges has been the inability to quickly identify the source of produce whenever export consignments are intercepted because of excessive pesticide residues or unregulated pesticides. “If you look at the market in the EU, the conditions given to us as a country, whenever there is an interception of the same, it’s difficult for our country to know where the crop comes from. If we have the system, which we do have now, we will be able to know particularly where the crop came from so that we can actually mitigate the problem that arose as a result of this interception. So, it will be a game changer for this country,” Serem said. AFA developed and upgraded the National Horticulture Traceability System to automate the tracking of fresh horticultural produce from farms in Kenya to international markets. The authority says the platform was designed to increase transparency and accountability in response to growing international food safety requirements. The system will also support other regulatory functions, including registration and licensing, import and export facilitation and data analytics. According to AFA, implementation of the platform is expected to increase market confidence, improve accountability, strengthen Kenya’s competitiveness in export markets and enable faster responses to food safety risks. Acting AFA Director General Calistus Kundu said the platform captures detailed information throughout the production process. “So, we are going to consider, by registering a farmer, the farmer’s farm and specific blocks that those crops are coming from. When the farmer registers the block, he also registers when he planted, the variety he planted, then all the horticultural practises in that farm, including weeding, spraying, fertilisation, as well as watering, and finally, harvesting,” said Kundu. The system will also record post-harvest activities, including transportation, grading and dispatch to markets. “So this is going to the level of knowing even who harvests the crop, and at what time, so that the buyer knows when this crop was harvested, when it was transported to the packhouse, and when it was graded, then finally dispatched to the market,” he said. According to Kundu, development of the platform has taken more than a decade and represents a major advancement for Kenya’s export horticulture industry, adding that it will be a game-changer in the export business, especially in high-end markets. The platform has been integrated with a mobile application to allow farmers to use it directly from their farms. Kundu said AFA has already trained many horticulture farmers ahead of the rollout. He added that the system will help protect compliant farmers from being penalised because of violations committed by others. “Today, if Farmer A is the one who sprayed a bad chemical, and they are in a group of 20 farmers, we might not be able to point out who really did this. So at the end of the day, we are banning all the farmers from exporting. But with this, if Farmer X is the one who sprayed, the rest shall continue exporting their crops to the market, and accessing better markets for that matter,” he said. The Director of the Horticultural Crops Directorate (HCD), Christine Chesaro, said exporters will drive the initial implementation of the system by registering all farms supplying produce for export. “The exporter is required to register all the sources of their produce. So, the exporter will make sure that he has mapped all the farms where he is bringing his produce from,” she said. According to Chesaro, the platform will improve oversight of export volumes and help eliminate produce sourced from unverified locations. “The situation we had before is that an exporter will show you a farm of 10 tonnes, but when you look at the returns of what they have exported, they will have exported 100 tonnes. And we don’t know where they got the rest. They probably got it from either Marikiti or somewhere where nobody knows how the crop was developed,” she said. The system incorporates farm mapping and geolocation technology, enabling regulators to link export permits directly to verified production areas. “The mapping will have to be done at the farm level. That is why the interaction with the farmer is a must. Because we must visit their farm, locate the geolocation and say in these 10 acres, they have planted French beans. And out of these French beans, we expect this tonnage. And that is the tonnage that we are going to process and nothing more for export,” Chesaro said. She added that the platform will eventually be expanded beyond exports. “The current rollout will start with the exporters. As soon as we are through with the exporters, we now move into the formal supermarkets. And then from there we now move to the informal,” she said. Industry players welcomed the launch, describing traceability as essential to maintaining Kenya’s competitiveness in international markets. Wanjohi Mukuria, Chairman of the Avocado Aggregators Association of Kenya, said stakeholders support the initiative but urged a phased implementation approach. “On this issue of traceability, we are completely behind it, and we feel it is the right way forward to be followed. We also need to train the farmers on what is expected from them, and not the farmers alone, even the exporters, even the marketing agents and everybody else involved must be put in the picture to know exactly what they are expected to do,” he said. Joseph Wagurah, Chairman of the Avocado Exporters Association, said the new platform creates a unified national system for tracing produce. “Previously, each company has been doing its own traceability system. Now it has been nationalized, so there will only be one traceability system to be able to trace the fruits right from the farm to the export market,” he said. According to Wagurah, the system will strengthen buyer confidence by enabling authorities to quickly identify the source of any consignment that fails to meet market requirements. “The advantage of that is that in the unlikely event of a rejection in the international market, which comes to KEPHIS, they will be able to trace the fruits and exactly get where they came from and then they can take action,” he said. Farmers and exporters also expect the platform to help tackle produce theft and reduce operational costs through digitisation. Juliana Tele, Export Manager at Karen Roses, said traceability would provide greater protection for growers. “The challenge we have been having as farmers on the ground is that if you cannot use the traceability system like this, your produce will be stolen. Avocado theft is really bad. It has made some growers really uproot the avocados because they wait for you when they almost ripen, they come and clear during the night, and you really go to a loss. And the way they are clearing, from what I understand from most of the farmers, they’ll destroy the trees because they are in a hurry. They destroy the tree completely, so it makes you go back to zero. So, we need the government to help us with some security.  So, this will be able to help us as the farmers to at least trace our produce all the way from the ground up to export,” she opined Tele also welcomed the shift to digital processing of permits and documentation. “I have been in the industry for 35 years myself. We used to carry a lot of paperwork, and it was so expensive, but now we will be able to do everything at our desk, and we’ll get the results. “We do not have to travel to carry the documents. This is paperless, and will reduce our cost.” As Kenya seeks to maintain access to premium export markets and strengthen compliance with international food safety standards, industry stakeholders believe the National Horticulture Traceability System will play a critical role in improving transparency, accountability and confidence in Kenyan horticultural produce from farm to market. KNCCI and KALRO pledge to Bridge Science and Business by Commercializing Agricultural Innovations. Home»Agriculture Policy»KNCCI and KALRO pledge to Bridge Science and Business by Commercializing Agricultural Innovations By Kimuri Mwangi For years, Kenyan scientists have developed improved crop varieties, livestock breeds and agricultural technologies capable of transforming farming and boosting incomes. Yet many of these innovations have struggled to move beyond research institutions and into the hands of farmers and businesses that need them most. That gap between research and the marketplace took center stage at the 2nd KALRO Scientific Conference and Innovation Expo held last week, where the Kenya National Chamber of Commerce and Industry (KNCCI) pledged to work with the Kenya Agricultural and Livestock Research Organization (KALRO) to commercialize innovations, connect them to markets and accelerate the growth of agribusiness across the country. Speaking during the 2nd KALRO Scientific Conference and Innovation Expo, KNCCI President Dr. Erick Rutto committed the chamber to supporting the commercialization of KALRO technologies and innovations, saying stronger collaboration between researchers and the private sector is essential for transforming agriculture into a more competitive and profitable industry. Dr. Rutto said KNCCI, through its nationwide network of 47 chambers and over 60,000 members, is uniquely positioned to connect agricultural innovations with markets and investors. Of the chamber’s membership, about 6,000 businesses have exported products, while 1,500 are currently active exporters. Notably, 80 percent of these exporters operate within the agricultural sector, underlining the industry’s central role in Kenya’s economy. “The theme for this event is innovation, collaboration and connecting research to industry. As the private sector, we have the market intelligence, we have the business people who are doing it, and the opportunities that have been created through the research that has been done here,” said Dr. Rutto. The KNCCI chief noted that Kenya’s success in several agricultural value chains is a direct result of sustained investment in research and innovation. He cited the tea sector, where Kenya remains the third-largest producer globally, and the floriculture industry, where the country is recognized as a leading exporter of cut flowers and roses. According to Dr Rutto, these achievements demonstrate the impact of research institutions such as KALRO on national productivity and export competitiveness. However, he argued that the next frontier lies in commercializing innovations and increasing value addition. Tea, for example, earns Kenya approximately Sh187 billion annually, yet about 95 percent of the commodity is exported in bulk form. He said this represents a missed opportunity to generate greater returns through processing and value-added products. “As the Kenya National Chamber of Commerce and Industry, our role is to see how we can get markets, especially for value-added products,” he said. “There is a number of research outputs here that can be able to process tea into tea products and other products from tea, and we can be able to get markets as a chamber of commerce.” He emphasized that KNCCI would actively work with KALRO to identify commercially viable innovations and help position them in domestic, regional and international markets. “We are going to collaborate with the research activities and the outputs that have come from here, and see how to market them,” he said. Dr. Rutto described agriculture as more than a traditional livelihood, calling it one of the strongest pillars of Kenya’s economy. According to KNCCI, agriculture contributes 22.47 percent of Kenya’s Gross Domestic Product (GDP), accounts for 65 percent of national exports, supports over 45 percent of the formal workforce and sustains about 70 percent of rural communities. These figures, he said, demonstrate why agricultural transformation should be treated as both an economic and business priority. “Every single day, millions of Kenyans wake up to till land that is shrinking in size, fighting increasingly unpredictable weather patterns, and chasing markets that feel entirely out of reach,” said Dr. Rutto. “Let us also be honest with ourselves: subsistence is a trap; commercialization is the escape. We cannot build a 2026 economy using 1970s tools.” He argued that Kenya must shift from traditional subsistence farming toward commercially driven, technology-enabled agricultural enterprises capable of competing globally. A key message was that agriculture should increasingly be viewed as a business opportunity driven by innovation, technology and entrepreneurship. He noted that Kenya’s reputation as Africa’s “Silicon Savannah” places the country in a strong position to integrate digital technologies into agriculture. Innovations such as mobile-based insurance solutions, Internet of Things (IoT) soil monitoring systems, localized weather forecasting and real-time yield mapping are already transforming agricultural productivity in large-scale farming operations. The challenge, he said, is ensuring these technologies reach smallholder farmers who form the backbone of Kenya’s agricultural sector. “There is an urgent need for everyone in this conference and in the agricultural sector to embrace horizontal scaling,” he said. “We need to integrate our brilliant, high-yield technologies proven by our large-scale commercial farms and put them into the hands of smallholder groups across our counties.” The KNCCI president pointed to mechanization as another major opportunity. Drawing from discussions at the Africa International Industrial Cooperation and Machinery Expo (AICMEC) 2026, he said modern agricultural equipment is becoming increasingly accessible to farmers operating on smaller and fragmented landholdings. Rather than relying solely on large-scale machinery, he highlighted the emergence of technologies specifically designed for African farming conditions, enabling smallholder farmers to improve productivity without prohibitive costs. Dr. Rutto also identified Kenya’s vast Arid and Semi-Arid Lands (ASALs) as an area with significant untapped potential. With more than 80 percent of Kenya’s landmass classified as ASAL, he said modern irrigation technologies, water recycling systems and climate-smart farming approaches could transform previously underutilized regions into productive agricultural zones. He cited examples from countries that have successfully used precision irrigation and water management technologies to overcome harsh climatic conditions and boost agricultural production. For Kenya, he said, these technologies present opportunities for investors, agribusinesses and farmers seeking to expand production in areas previously considered unsuitable for intensive agriculture. Kenya, it emerged, is sitting on substantial opportunities in livestock exports but continues to face production constraints. According to KNCCI, there is demand for approximately five million cattle and sheep from one market and an additional one million animals from another international market. However, local farmers are struggling to meet export requirements due to challenges related to breeding, quality standards and production volumes. He said collaboration between KNCCI and KALRO could help bridge this gap by providing farmers with improved breeds, better production systems and market-oriented training. The Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) market alone, he noted, imports 90 percent of its meat requirements and has experienced a 20 percent shortage of meat and meat products over the last five years. “This gap can be filled by the research that we are having here and the private sector within Kenya,” he said. While market access often receives significant attention, Dr. Rutto argued that Kenya’s biggest challenge lies in meeting international standards consistently. “The issue is not the market,” he said. “The issue is about the standards, the consistency and the quality.” Exporters frequently struggle to supply the required quality and quantity within specified timelines, limiting the country’s ability to capitalize on existing demand. Through closer collaboration between KALRO and KNCCI, he said these challenges can be addressed by linking farmers to modern technologies, improved seeds, superior livestock breeds and practical business solutions. To accelerate commercialization and competitiveness, Dr. Rutto proposed a four-point strategy. The first is increasing value addition to reduce the export of raw commodities such as tea, coffee and macadamia. According to KNCCI, local processing and agro-industrialization can increase commodity values by up to 400 percent. Second, he called for reforms in agribusiness education through short-course certifications in agricultural data science, drone piloting, robotics maintenance and agribusiness financial engineering. Third, he advocated mandatory skills transfer whenever international agricultural machinery is procured, ensuring local technicians acquire the expertise needed to operate and maintain advanced technologies. Finally, he encouraged the formation of aggregated cooperatives that would allow smallholder farmers to collectively access mechanization services and benefit from economies of scale. Dr. Rutto outlined several initiatives already being implemented by KNCCI to support agricultural innovation and business growth. Among them is the Jiinue Growth Programme, which provides affordable financing and business development support to Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs), including agribusinesses. The programme enables agricultural enterprises to invest in irrigation systems, modern machinery, value-addition technologies and digital solutions aimed at improving productivity and competitiveness. KNCCI is also implementing the Climate Smart Dairy Project, which promotes modern dairy technologies, climate-resilient feed production, efficient water management and digital record-keeping systems to help farmers increase milk production while adapting to climate change. Additionally, the chamber has intensified trade and investment missions to countries such as Saudi Arabia and Namibia, creating new opportunities for agricultural exports, technology transfer, investment partnerships and knowledge exchange. These engagements expose Kenyan agribusinesses to advanced mechanization technologies, smart irrigation systems and innovative value-chain solutions that can be adapted locally. As KALRO showcased scientific innovations and technologies during the conference, Dr. Rutto emphasized that the true measure of success will be the ability to convert research outcomes into profitable enterprises that improve livelihoods and create jobs. He pledged that KNCCI would continue serving as a bridge between researchers, investors, entrepreneurs and markets. “Those are the commitments that we have been doing with KALRO, and we want to proceed and scale it up to the next level,” he said. “As a chamber, we will continue to act as a bridge to technology transfer, the cheerleader to local innovation, while providing market intelligence to guide your ideas and at the same time opening markets to your produce locally, regionally and globally.” His message reflected a growing consensus that the future of Kenya’s agriculture will depend not only on scientific breakthroughs, but also on how effectively those innovations are commercialized, scaled and integrated into competitive agribusiness value chains. Kenya Targets 150,000 Metric Tonnes of Coffee by 2028. Home»Agriculture Policy»Kenya Targets 150,000 Metric Tonnes of Coffee by 2028 By Kimuri Mwangi Kenya has set an ambitious target of increasing coffee production to 150,000 metric tonnes by 2028, surpassing the country’s historical peak output, as the government accelerates reforms and investments to revitalise the sector and improve farmers’ earnings. Principal Secretary for the State Department for Cooperatives Patrick Kilemi said the government is determined to elevate Kenya’s standing in the global coffee industry through increased production, stronger cooperative societies, improved governance and enhanced market access. Speaking during the East Africa Coffee Markets and Conference (EACMC) Breakfast Meeting in Nairobi, Kilemi said Kenya must move beyond being a minor player in the international coffee trade and become a leading reference point in global coffee markets. “On the global coffee map, we do not want to be a dot; we want to be a number that will be referenced,” Kilemi said, noting that countries such as Ethiopia and Uganda have established strong positions in coffee exports within Africa. He said the government’s production target of 150,000 metric tonnes by 2028 reflects a broader strategy to restore the sector’s growth trajectory and strengthen Kenya’s competitiveness in international markets. To support the expansion, Kilemi said the government is scaling up interventions across the coffee value chain, including the distribution of seedlings and support for new coffee-growing areas. “We want to make sure that every farmer who digs a coffee hole gets a seedling at the right time,” he said. The PS noted that the government is aligning seedling distribution with planting seasons across different regions, including the Western and North Rift regions during the March-April rains and the Mount Kenya region during the October-November planting season. He added that coffee cultivation has expanded significantly beyond traditional growing zones, with 35 of Kenya’s 47 counties now growing coffee. According to Kilemi, cooperative societies remain central to the success of the coffee industry, given that the majority of smallholder farmers market their produce through cooperatives. “Coffee remains one of Kenya’s most important agricultural commodities and a major source of livelihood for hundreds of thousands of smallholder farmers. Most of these farmers market their coffee through cooperative societies, making cooperatives the backbone of the coffee value chain,” he said. The government, he noted, is implementing reforms aimed at improving governance within coffee cooperatives, strengthening farmer representation and enhancing transparency in coffee marketing systems to ensure farmers receive a fair share of the value generated from their produce. Among the key interventions is the digitization of cooperative operations to improve efficiency, reduce transaction costs, strengthen traceability systems and provide farmers with timely information. Kilemi said digital solutions are increasingly becoming necessary as international markets demand greater transparency and accountability throughout agricultural supply chains. He also challenged coffee farmers and cooperatives to invest in quality improvement, sustainability and compliance with emerging market requirements. Referring to the conference theme,“From Farm to Flavor: Quality, Compliance and Market Growth,”the PS said global buyers are placing greater emphasis on quality, traceability, sustainability standards and responsible production practices. He said this requires deliberate investment in improved agronomic practices, better post-harvest handling systems, stronger certification mechanisms and climate-resilient production methods. Beyond production, Kilemi emphasized the need for value addition, branding and market differentiation to enable farmers and cooperatives to capture a larger share of the returns generated from Kenya’s globally recognized coffee. The government, he said, will continue supporting the sector through policy reforms, capacity building programmes, infrastructure development and strategic partnerships. Kilemi also highlighted the importance of regional integration, arguing that East African countries could strengthen their bargaining power, attract investment and unlock new markets by working together to promote the region’s coffee as a premium global brand. He further underscored the need to attract young people and women into coffee production, processing, marketing and innovation, saying the future of the industry depends on creating profitable opportunities for the next generation. The remarks were made during the launch of the East Africa Coffee Markets & Conference (EACMC) 2026, which officially kicked off with a Coffee Market Access & Partnership Breakfast that brought together policymakers, investors, development partners, diplomatic missions and industry players from across the coffee value chain. The conference to be held in October is being repositioned as a regional market access platform aimed at connecting producers, cooperatives, exporters, buyers and investors while creating tangible business opportunities through trade linkages, investment promotion and knowledge exchange. Speaking ahead of the conference, Smart Farmer Africa CEO and EACMC convenor  Bernadette Murgor said the platform is designed to address one of the biggest challenges facing the coffee sector, connecting producers to markets. “East Africa Coffee Markets & Conference 2026 is more than a conference; it is a strategic market access platform designed to connect producers, cooperatives, exporters, buyers and investors through meaningful business opportunities,” Murgor said. She noted that while East Africa produces some of the world’s finest coffees, many producers and cooperatives remain disconnected from potential buyers, market intelligence and financing opportunities. “A farmer may produce excellent coffee but remain invisible to the market. A cooperative may have strong volumes but lack the right buyer networks, market intelligence or presentation materials. A buyer may be interested in East African coffee but may not know where to begin,” she said. According to Murgor, the conference seeks to bridge these gaps by fostering structured engagement among producers, buyers, financial institutions, development partners, and diplomatic missions, ultimately strengthening the competitiveness of East African coffee in regional and international markets. Yatta Cotton Farmers Receive Pesticides and Modern Sprayers. Home»Agriculture Policy»Yatta Cotton Farmers Receive Pesticides and Modern Sprayers More than 1,000 cotton farmers in Ndalani and neighbouring areas of Yatta Sub-County in Machakos County have received pesticides and modern spraying machines under a government-backed initiative aimed at increasing the production of climate-resilient crops in the semi-arid region. The support, facilitated through the Agriculture and Food Authority (AFA), benefited farmers from the Ndalani, Kisiiki, Kiwanzani, Nthungululu, Mbembani, Kivingoni and Kakongo clusters. The programme is designed to reduce production costs, strengthen pest management and improve cotton yields in an area where the crop has emerged as a dependable source of income due to its resilience to harsh climatic conditions. Speaking during the distribution exercise, Francis Kilango, Director of the National Cotton Growers Union and Chairman of the Ndalani Cotton Farmers Cooperative Society, called on leaders to invest in climate-smart agriculture as a way of improving livelihoods in the region. “Leaders, whether elected or aspiring, should support farmers to embrace resilient crops such as cotton and sunflower. These crops perform well even in areas with unreliable rainfall and offer better returns to farmers,” said Kilango. He said cotton has become a key economic driver for thousands of households across Yatta, offering farmers a reliable source of income even during extended periods of drought. Farmers welcomed the intervention, noting that the generator-powered spraying machines will improve the efficiency of pest control operations, lower labour costs and contribute to increased productivity. Led by Daniel Nzioki, the farmers also appealed for continued government support through subsidized farm inputs, extension services and access to modern farming technologies to sustain the ongoing revival of cotton farming and strengthen household incomes across Machakos County. At the same time, the farmers expressed concern over poor germination rates from cotton seeds distributed during the previous planting season, saying the problem resulted in significant losses for many growers. “We are appealing to the government to provide Bt cotton seeds before the October planting season. Quality seed will increase yields, reduce production costs and protect farmers from unnecessary losses,” said Nzioki. Cotton farming has continued to gain traction in Yatta as more farmers turn to drought-tolerant crops such as cotton and sunflower in response to changing weather patterns. The crops are increasingly complementing maize and beans, which remain the area’s primary food crops. According to farmers, production has improved significantly in recent years following renewed government support, better access to markets and increased awareness of cotton’s economic potential. These developments have encouraged more growers to adopt the crop as a sustainable source of income in the region’s challenging climatic conditions. 4K Clubs Inspire a New Generation of Kenyan Farmers. Home»Agriculture Policy»4K Clubs Inspire a New Generation of Kenyan Farmers By Kimuri Mwangi Young learners from 10 primary schools took centre stage at Egerton University’s main campus during a 4K Club Farmers Field Day, demonstrating agricultural knowledge and practical farming skills that stakeholders say are critical to securing Kenya’s future food systems and nurturing a new generation of agribusiness professionals. The event, held on June 5, 2026, was the result of a partnership by the Ministry of Agriculture and Livestock Development, aak-GROW (CropLife Kenya), Egerton University and other partners to support the growing impact of the 4K Club programme (Kuungana, Kufanya, Kusaidia Kenya), which seeks to instil agricultural knowledge, innovation and entrepreneurship among young people from an early age. At the heart of the field day were the students themselves. Having been allocated plots at Egerton University, the learners planted, managed and monitored a variety of crops, applying recommended agronomic practices under the guidance of agricultural experts and mentors. During the event, they confidently explained the farming methods they used, the challenges they encountered and the lessons they learned throughout the growing season. The practical exposure transformed the students from passive learners into knowledgeable young farmers capable of discussing crop management, food production, environmental stewardship and the responsible use of agricultural technologies. Unlike conventional classroom-based lessons, the initiative places students directly in the field, where they gain hands-on experience in every stage of crop production. The demonstration plots featured crops representing different nutritional categories, including starches, vegetables and protein-rich foods. Organizers said the exercise was designed not only to teach farming techniques but also to deepen learners’ understanding of food security, nutrition and sustainable agricultural production. Stakeholders attending the event were impressed by the students’ ability to explain their farming practices and the principles behind them. According to aak-GROW Chief Executive Officer Joel Mutai, the field demonstrations provided clear evidence that the learners had acquired practical skills and understood the importance of producing safe food through responsible farming practices. “You have seen the kind of crops they have grown using agronomic practices as required,” said Mutai. “The children really appreciated the programme. It came out very clearly in the way they explained how agriculture should be practised safely and how to make crops grow better.” The field day demonstrated that the learners had moved beyond simply planting crops. They had become informed participants capable of discussing modern agricultural practices, crop protection, environmental responsibility and food safety. By managing their own plots, the students gained practical experience that organizers believe positions them to become future agricultural professionals, agribusiness entrepreneurs and commercial farmers. The initiative comes at a time when Kenya faces growing concerns over an ageing farming population and the need to attract more young people into agriculture. Charles Lusweti, Head of the Youth in Agriculture Unit at the Ministry of Agriculture and Livestock Development, said the 4K Club programme is helping bridge the gap between an ageing farmer population and the country’s rapidly growing youth population. “We are inculcating the practice of agriculture from an early age so that we bridge the gap of the ageing farmer and the growing population,” Lusweti said. He noted that the programme is already operating in all 47 counties, with approximately 2,700 registered clubs involving nearly 300,000 learners. According to Lusweti, exposing young people to agriculture early creates opportunities not only for food production but also for future employment across the agricultural value chain. “This is a great opportunity to showcase how these learners can be important not only to the community but to the government as a whole,” he said. “Getting to entice them this early will create many benefits in terms of realizing the goal of food security and nutrition, as well as getting many of them employed within the agricultural sector.” The Ministry rebranded and relaunched the 4K Clubs in 2021, introducing a modern approach that incorporates technology, innovation and agribusiness opportunities. Learners are now exposed to emerging technologies such as drones, digital agriculture and other modern farming innovations, helping change perceptions that agriculture is limited to traditional farming practices. “You don’t need to be tilling the land with your hands,” Lusweti said. “There are other areas where you can involve yourself. There are technologies, drones and digitalization in agriculture.” Mutai noted that the average age of a farmer in Kenya is estimated at 64 years, making youth engagement increasingly important. “The future becomes uncertain if we do not bring young people into agriculture,” he said. He described the 4K Club programme as a practical strategy for ensuring future generations remain interested in agriculture, food production and nutrition. A key driver behind the programme is the Stewardship Mashinani initiative spearheaded by aak-GROW. The initiative was developed to address gaps in the adoption of safe agricultural practices, particularly among farmers at the grassroots level. It is a community-driven behaviour change programme designed to promote responsible pest management and the safe use of crop protection technologies through structures that farmers interact with regularly. These include community health promoters, schools, religious institutions, farmer groups, cooperatives, agro-dealers and local community forums. The incorporation of 4K Clubs into the programme reflects a deliberate effort to use young people as catalysts for long-term behavioural change. “The youth represent a significant and influential segment of the population with the potential to drive meaningful change in society,” aak-GROW noted. Through the initiative, students are introduced to responsible pesticide use, environmental stewardship and sustainable agricultural practices while still at a formative stage of learning. “It is a very important initiative where we are now inculcating the culture of good agronomic practices in the young ones,” Mutai said. “As they grow, they will appreciate agriculture and become ambassadors in their families in terms of how pesticides and other agricultural technologies are used.” He said the programme is built on broad-based partnerships that bring together government institutions, farmers, community leaders, religious groups and young people in promoting safer and more sustainable agriculture. “The Mashinani Initiative is a concerted effort to make sure everybody is involved, including leaders, farmers, religious groups and young people,” he said. Mutai added that schools provide a powerful platform for spreading messages on safe pesticide use, environmental stewardship and good agronomic practices, as learners often share their knowledge with families and communities. The initiative also aligns with growing collaboration between agriculture and environmental stakeholders, including efforts to strengthen a “One Health” approach linking food production, environmental conservation and climate resilience. One of the most notable outcomes of the field day was the confidence with which students communicated agricultural knowledge. Learners demonstrated an understanding of crop management practices, safety procedures and sustainable production methods, underscoring the value of experiential learning. For CropLife Africa Middle East Chief Executive Officer Stella Simiyu, this demonstrated the power of investing in young people as agents of change. “If you can explain a technology to a child and they understand it, when they go back to their siblings and neighbours, the message spreads much faster,” she said. Simiyu argued that equipping young people with agricultural knowledge creates a multiplier effect that extends beyond schools and reaches households and entire communities. “We are looking at behaviour change,” she said. “If we build this generation by fortifying them with knowledge and skills in agriculture and exposing them to available technologies, they will change society for us.” Egerton University played a central role in the initiative by providing land, mentorship and technical support that enabled learners to gain practical agricultural experience within one of Kenya’s leading agricultural institutions. The students were guided by lecturers and specialists from the university’s Faculty of Education and Community Studies, particularly the Department of Agricultural Education and Extension, throughout the growing season. The practical approach allowed learners to move beyond theory and become active participants in food production while interacting directly with agricultural professionals. Egerton University Vice-Chancellor Prof. Isaac Ongubo Kibwage said the field day offered an opportunity to celebrate the enthusiasm demonstrated by the learners and the practical knowledge they had gained. “The enthusiasm they have shown in tending their plots is remarkable,” said Prof. Kibwage. “The produce will help demonstrate to other learners how even a small piece of land can contribute to feeding families and communities.” He said the initiative teaches responsibility, food production and the value of sharing within communities while transforming agricultural concepts into practical learning experiences. The Vice-Chancellor praised participating schools and the university’s Faculty of Education and Community Studies for helping transform the plots into living classrooms where students learn by doing. Prof. Kibwage revealed that interest in agriculture-related courses at Egerton University has been steadily increasing after years of low enrolment associated with poor performance in agriculture-related subject clusters at the secondary school level. “We have seen improved uptake and, in some cases, even oversubscription because of capacity limitations,” he said. He attributed the renewed interest to changing perceptions about agriculture and growing recognition of its role in livelihoods, employment and food security. According to the Vice-Chancellor, previous generations often discouraged young people from pursuing agriculture by portraying farming as an occupation for those who had not excelled academically. However, attitudes are changing as more people appreciate the sector’s importance to both household and national economies. Prof. Kibwage also linked the 4K Club initiative to broader challenges posed by rapid urbanization, population growth and shrinking arable land. He warned that continued subdivision of productive agricultural land poses a significant threat to future food production and called for more responsible land-use planning. At the same time, he said the students’ demonstration plots illustrate how efficient and intensive farming can help address food security challenges even in limited spaces. “What these young learners have demonstrated is that you can produce food even on a small piece of land,” he said. The Vice-Chancellor pointed to kitchen gardens, vertical farming systems and balcony gardening as practical solutions for urban households seeking to supplement their food needs. He said such innovations will become increasingly important as urban populations continue to grow and agricultural land becomes more constrained. For organizers, the field day was about much more than showcasing crops. It was a demonstration of how practical agricultural education can equip young people with skills that position them for future success in agribusiness and food production. By managing their own plots, making production decisions and applying modern farming practices, the learners are developing expertise that could eventually translate into commercial farming enterprises, agricultural careers and leadership roles within the sector. As Kenya seeks solutions to food insecurity, youth unemployment and an ageing farmer population, stakeholders believe programmes such as the 4K Clubs and aak-GROW’s Stewardship Mashinani initiative are helping lay the foundation for a new generation of knowledgeable, technology-driven and environmentally conscious farmers. The students who showcased their work at Egerton University may still be in primary school today, but their confidence in the field, understanding of modern farming practices and ability to articulate agricultural concepts offered a glimpse into the future of Kenyan agriculture, one in which the country’s next crop of agribusiness experts is already taking root. Kenya Enhances Policy Monitoring to Accelerate Sustainable Agriculture Transformation. Home»Agriculture Policy»Kenya Enhances Policy Monitoring to Accelerate Sustainable Agriculture Transformation By Kimuri Mwangi Kenya is intensifying efforts to transform its agricultural sector through the implementation of the National Agroecology Strategy for Food System Transformation (2024–2033), with stakeholders now focusing on strengthening monitoring systems to ensure the policy delivers sustainable results. This renewed push was highlighted during a high-level training held in Nairobi from May 12 to 15, 2026, organized by the Association for Strengthening Agricultural Research in Eastern and Central Africa (ASARECA) under its Comprehensive Africa Agriculture Development Programme ex-Pillar IV (CAADP-XP4) project, funded by the European Union and managed by IFAD. The training brought together government officials, policymakers, researchers, county representatives, farmer organizations, non-governmental organizations, and policy think tanks to build capacity in applying the ASARECA Policy Manager Tool for the joint analysis and monitoring of Kenya’s newly developed agroecology strategy. ASARECA, an intergovernmental organization representing 15 member countries in Eastern and Central Africa, is mandated to coordinate agricultural research for development initiatives aimed at improving productivity, resilience, and sustainable agricultural production across the region. Its strategic focus includes partnerships and capacity development, technology transfer, policy environment enhancement, and knowledge management, all aimed at accelerating agricultural transformation. Speaking during the Nairobi workshop, Dr. Alice Nyagah, Agricultural Engineer at Kenya’s Ministry of Agriculture and Livestock Development (MoALD), said the country developed the National Agroecology Strategy in response to major shifts in food systems and the need for a coordinated approach to sustainable agriculture. “Kenya has developed the National Agroecology Strategy for Food System Transformation. So today we are here at ASARECA Policy Management Tool training to train stakeholders, government officials, on how to monitor the progress in the implementation of the strategy,” Dr. Nyagah said. She noted that the strategy was formulated to unify key actors across the agricultural value chain, including government, farmers, producers, and traders, in managing production and distribution systems more effectively. “This training will help us also to improve our capabilities, strengthen our reporting and data collection,” she added. The ASARECA Policy Manager Tool, first developed in 2023 and later refined through stakeholder consultations in Nairobi and Kampala, serves as a virtual dashboard that evaluates how well agricultural policies are formulated and implemented. According to ASARECA Programme Officer for Policy, Julian Barungi, the tool is designed to identify implementation gaps early enough to prevent policy failure and maximize intended outcomes. “We are here in Nairobi for a training on the ASARECA policy management tool that we have rolled out here in Kenya to analyse Kenya’s national agroecology strategy. The idea is really to identify the challenges so far in the implementation, any obstacles that might be there in the implementation, so that we can correct any challenges at the right time and avoid policy failure,” said Barungi. The training is particularly significant because Kenya’s agroecology strategy is still in its early implementation phase, creating an opportunity to identify barriers and apply corrective measures before major setbacks occur. Participants were trained to use the tool to assess policy effectiveness, review implementation progress, identify challenges, extract lessons, and formulate actionable recommendations. Expected outputs include evidence-based recommendations to improve implementation, development of a joint action plan with clear timelines and institutional responsibilities, policy briefs for decision-makers, and strengthened stakeholder capacity for independent policy analysis. Moses Odeke, ASARECA’s CAADP-XP4 Coordinator, emphasized that policy frameworks are critical for the successful deployment of agricultural innovations, including agroecology technologies. “For us to be able to do this appropriately, we need to build the capacities of our stakeholders. In the same vein, we need to have an enabling policy environment. Once we’ve done the review, we’ll then be able to come up with key recommendations that will be turned into action,” Odeke said. ASARECA officials say the Policy Manager Tool not only supports policy evaluation but also promotes best practices in policy design across member states by allowing countries to compare policy performance and learn from one another. Achilley Ssebwana, ASARECA Programme Officer for Information Systems, said the tool provides governments and institutions with a structured framework to assess whether policies are meeting their intended objectives and whether critical stakeholders, including farmers and scientists, are fulfilling their roles. “This policy management tool comes in at that level, where, if at all, there is an existing policy that has been set up, we need to evaluate that process, either at the time when it has been completed or when it is under implementation or when it is reaching its end of life,” Ssebwana opined. Experts attending the workshop also underscored the strategic importance of Kenya’s agroecology policy in balancing agricultural transformation with sustainability. Tim Njagi, Spatial Modelling Specialist at the Africa Network of Agricultural Policy Research Institutes (ANAPRI), said the strategy is essential for ensuring agricultural growth does not compromise natural resources. “When you look at our development goals, we want to grow agriculture, we want to transform agriculture, but it has to be sustainable. So, the agroecology strategy basically comes to help us in ensuring that even as we are pursuing transformation, we are doing it in a way that will be sustainable, not just for this generation, but for future generations. Opined Njagi. “This training deepens the understanding of what is contained in the strategy. The challenge we have in many African countries is that policy processes take a long time, from the time you start developing your policy to the time it’s enacted and you start implementation.” Kenya’s National Agroecology Strategy aims to support sustainable food systems by promoting environmentally sound agricultural practices while addressing food security, climate resilience, and long-term economic development. The Nairobi training marks a critical step in operationalizing the strategy by equipping institutions with practical tools for implementation oversight, accountability, and adaptive policy management. As Kenya advances its agroecology agenda, the collaboration between government institutions, regional bodies like ASARECA, and development partners is expected to play a pivotal role in ensuring the strategy achieves its intended impact and contributes to broader agricultural transformation across Eastern and Central Africa. Home»Agriculture Policy»Cashew nuts lead as Tanzania’s Commercial Crop Output Hits 1.6 Million Tons The production of commercial crops in Tanzania has continued to improve, reaching 1,599,945.66 tonnes in the 2025/2026 agricultural year, equivalent to 71.9 percent. This marks a 10.0 percent increase compared to the 1,451,694.11 tonnes produced in the 2024/2025 agricultural year. These figures were presented by the Minister of Agriculture, Hon. Daniel Chongolo (MP), while delivering the Ministry’s Revenue and Expenditure Estimates speech for the 2026/2027 financial year on April 28, 2026, in Dodoma. Hon. Chongolo explained that production of various crops has shown a positive trend, with cashew nuts leading at 617,683.77 tonnes, cotton at 222,014 tonnes, and pyrethrum at 2,780 tonnes. Additionally, coffee production reached 74,663.60 tonnes, tobacco 185,776 tonnes, and tea 11,631.48 tonnes. Other crops included sisal at 64,321.77 tonnes, sugarcane at 410,979.04 tonnes, and cocoa at 10,096 tonnes. While outlining plans to boost production further, Hon. Chongolo said the Ministry aims to increase output from 1,599,945.66 tonnes in 2025/2026 to 2,118,000 tonnes in the 2026/2027 agricultural year. Tanzania Launches Integrity Campaign to Clean Up Cooperative Sector. Home»Agriculture Policy»Tanzania Launches Integrity Campaign to Clean Up Cooperative Sector Tanzania Agriculture Minister Daniel Chongolo has announced that the government is in the final stages of establishing a collaborative framework with the Prevention and Combating of Corruption Bureau (PCCB) and the Ministry of Home Affairs to set up specialized police desks dedicated to handling cooperative society matters across the country. Speaking in Dodoma, during the launch of the “Protect Cooperatives, Choose Integrity” campaign, Chongolo said the initiative is aimed at strengthening accountability, curbing corruption and embezzlement, and ensuring that those involved in malpractice face legal action without favouritism. He stressed that the government will not tolerate leaders of cooperative societies who misuse members’ resources, citing the case of Sikonge AMCOS, which lost more than Sh1.2 billion due to financial mismanagement. He also condemned fraudulent practices in crop purchasing, including the manipulation of weighing scales. Chongolo noted that the cooperative sector contributes more than Sh6 trillion to the economy, underscoring the need for robust management systems to safeguard members’ interests and promote farmers’ economic growth. “The government will not allow a few dishonest individuals to sabotage the cooperative movement, which remains a vital pillar of farmers’ economic empowerment,” he said. Meanwhile, Deputy Minister for Home Affairs Ayoub Mohamed Mahmoud affirmed his ministry’s readiness to work closely with the agriculture sector in strengthening security and combating criminal activities within cooperative societies. On his part, Ghati Chomeke, a representative of the Parliamentary Standing Committee on Agriculture, Industry, Trade and Fisheries, emphasized the need to expand education for cooperative members on market access and crop value to improve commercial decision-making and boost incomes.
