Kenya Coffee Direct Sales Rake In USD 49 Million and Beat Auction by 20%. Home»Kilimo General»Kenya Coffee Direct Sales Rake In USD 49 Million and Beat Auction by 20% By Henry Kinyua Figures from the AFA Coffee Directorate show that Direct Sales moved 6,066.31 metric tonnes of clean Kenyan coffee between October 2025 and June 2026, earning USD 49.24 million at a weighted average of USD 406 per 50-kg bag. Set that against the auction’s season average of USD 339, and the arithmetic speaks plainly: coffee sold directly fetched USD 67 more per bag, a premium of nearly 20% for growers. The channel did not start the year in a hurry. October opened with a modest 98,663 kg worth USD 0.84 million, barely a warm-up lap. November nearly doubled that to 197,978 kg (USD 1.74 million), December more than doubled it again to 485,913 kg (USD 4.26 million), and the new year kept climbing: 730,662 kg (USD 6.07 million) in January and 754,650 kg (USD 6.81 million) in February. Then came March, and March did not come quietly. The month moved 1,952,915 kg, nearly a third of the entire season’s direct volume, and banked USD 15.98 million, the highest monthly haul of the year. April kept a respectable pace at 1,163,938 kg (USD 8.86 million) before the season eased into its tail: 348,919 kg (USD 2.23 million) in May and 332,670 kg (USD 2.44 million) in June. From October through March, the average never dipped below USD 400 per bag, peaking at USD 451.34 in February. May brought the season’s soft spot at USD 320.06 before June recovered to USD 366.97, a reminder that even the premium channel obeys the calendar. If Direct Sales were a football league, the United Kingdom and Switzerland would have wrapped up the title by Christmas. The UK took 2.43 million kg (40.05% of volume, USD 20.61 million), Switzerland 2.35 million kg (38.81%, USD 16.03 million). Between them, the two markets absorbed 79% of everything Kenya sold. Germany directly followed at a distant 391,728 kg (6.46%) and the United States at 237,823 kg (3.92%), with 29 other destinations sharing the remainder, none claiming more than 2%. The price table, however, belonged to the small buyers. Jordan paid a remarkable USD 926 per bag, more than double the season average and comfortably the best rate any destination offered. Japan followed at USD 686, Denmark at USD 675 and Hong Kong at USD 647. At the other end sat Somalia at USD 249, proof that “direct” does not automatically mean “dear”; it means the price you negotiate. What should the farmer read into this? First, that the premium is real and persistent: a near 20% price advantage over the auction, sustained across a full nine months, is not a rounding error; it is a strategy. Cooperatives with the quality, the certifications and the paperwork to court direct buyers are leaving money on the table if they do not. Second, concentration is a comfort and a risk in equal measure. The UK and Switzerland are loyal, deep-pocketed customers, but a channel where two markets take 79% is calling for diversification and insurance. Third, the specialty fringe pays like a specialty fringe. Jordan at USD 925, Japan at USD 685, Denmark at USD 674,  are small volumes at extraordinary prices, and they signal an appetite for distinctive Kenyan lots that our marketing agents and cooperatives have barely begun to farm. Add the direct channel’s USD 49 million to the auction’s USD 245 million, and Kenya’s coffee marketed through both windows this season stands at 42 million kg worth USD 294 million, at a blended average of USD 348.39 per bag. The auction remains the backbone. Cooperatives Dismiss Claims Government Will Use SACCO Savings to Fund Infrastructure Projects. Home»Kilimo General»Cooperatives Dismiss Claims Government Will Use SACCO Savings to Fund Infrastructure Projects By Kimuri Mwangi The Cooperative Alliance of Kenya (CAK) has moved to reassure millions of Savings and Credit Cooperative Society (SACCO) members that their savings are not at risk, dismissing as false and legally unfounded claims that the government intends to channel members’ funds into financing long-term infrastructure projects. The assurance follows the circulation of reports on social media and some mainstream media platforms alleging that proposed amendments to Kenya’s cooperative laws would allow the government to access SACCO savings for infrastructure development. CAK says the claims have misrepresented the contents of the Cooperative Societies Bill and the SACCO Societies (Amendment) Bill, causing unnecessary concern among members. Addressing a press briefing on Friday, CAK Chief Executive Officer Daniel Marube said neither of the two proposed laws contains any provision permitting the government to seize or compel SACCOs to invest members’ savings in infrastructure bonds. “The claims circulating on social media and some mainstream media platforms have no basis in law,” Marube said. “They have caused unnecessary anxiety among our members, yet there is absolutely nothing in the legislation that permits the government to take members’ savings for infrastructure financing.” Marube said the Cooperative Societies Bill currently before Parliament is the culmination of nearly 14 years of consultations involving cooperative stakeholders and has now reached the mediation stage before enactment. He added that the SACCO Societies (Amendment) Bill has also undergone extensive review following submissions by cooperative leaders and members and will still be subjected to further public participation once it reaches the Senate. Among the key reforms proposed in the amendment bill is the establishment of unions of SACCOs, allowing institutions with shared interests to collaborate and improve operational efficiency. According to Marube, the unions would enable SACCOs to share services such as legal support, auditing and technology, lower operating costs through economies of scale, and facilitate inter-lending among member SACCOs to strengthen liquidity. “The so-called ‘super SACCO’ is simply a union of SACCOs working together. It is not an institution designed to pool members’ savings for government projects,” he said. The amendment bill also proposes the establishment of a SACCO Deposit Guarantee Fund to protect members’ deposits should a SACCO become insolvent. Marube compared the proposal to the Kenya Deposit Insurance Fund, which safeguards deposits held in commercial banks. He said the guarantee fund would function as an insurance mechanism to compensate members in the event of institutional failure. Responding to concerns that SACCOs could eventually be required to invest in long-term government securities, Marube said such decisions can only be made by members themselves through Annual General Meetings (AGMs). “No government authority can compel a SACCO to invest in infrastructure bonds. Investment decisions are made democratically by members during AGMs,” he said. Marube acknowledged that many SACCOs already invest surplus liquidity in short-term government securities, including 91-day Treasury Bills, but stressed that those investments are intended solely for liquidity management rather than long-term financing. He emphasized that the primary role of SACCOs is to provide affordable credit to members, warning that tying up funds in long-term investments would undermine that mandate. The proposed legislative reforms also seek to strengthen governance within the cooperative sector by requiring all deposit-taking SACCOs to be regulated by the Sacco Societies Regulatory Authority (SASRA), introducing mandatory vetting of directors before assuming office, accrediting auditors, and imposing tougher penalties for fraud. Marube said officials found guilty of embezzling members’ funds would be barred from serving in any cooperative institution, prosecuted, and have their assets recovered to compensate affected members. “We want to make the consequences so severe that anyone contemplating stealing cooperative funds will think twice,” Marube said. He also sought to dispel misconceptions surrounding the cooperative sector’s reported asset base of more than Sh1.3 trillion, saying the figure does not represent cash lying idle in bank accounts. Instead, he said, the assets largely comprise loans already advanced to members and other investments financed through SACCO lending. “People imagine there is Sh1.3 trillion sitting in bank accounts. That is not the case. Those assets are in homes, businesses, motor vehicles, school fees, medical expenses and other investments financed through member loans,” he said. Marube urged cooperative members to ignore misinformation and avoid sharing unverified reports that could undermine confidence in the cooperative movement. “If there were any attempt by the government MSEA, SNV Empower More Than 500 Uasin Gishu Entrepreneurs Through Policy Awareness Forums. Home»Kilimo General»MSEA, SNV Empower More Than 500 Uasin Gishu Entrepreneurs Through Policy Awareness Forums By Kimuri Mwangi More than 500 entrepreneurs in Uasin Gishu County have been equipped with knowledge on proposed policy reforms, business support programmes and financing opportunities through a series of policy awareness forums organised by the Micro and Small Enterprises Authority (MSEA) and the Development Organisation SNV  under the Inclusive Youth Business for Africa (IYBA) SEED Project. The initiative culminated in its sixth and final forum at EldoHub in Soy Sub-County on Monday, where more than 60 micro, small and medium enterprise (MSME) operators joined representatives from government agencies, financial institutions, innovation hubs, universities and the private sector to discuss the future of Kenya’s MSME sector. The forums focused on creating awareness of the Draft MSME Policy 2025 and the proposed Micro and Small Enterprises (MSE) Amendment Bill 2025, while encouraging entrepreneurs to actively participate in shaping policies that affect their businesses. Speaking during the forum, SNV, IYBA-SEED Project Manager Nduta Ndirangu, said the engagement marked the final phase of the current collaboration between SNV and MSEA in Uasin Gishu County. She said the initiative was designed to ensure that entrepreneurs understand proposed policy reforms, available government programmes, and business support services that can help them establish, expand, and sustain their enterprises. Ndirangu observed that entrepreneurship can only thrive within a supportive ecosystem backed by enabling policies, accessible financing, quality business development services and innovation. She noted that the European Union-funded IYBA SEED Project seeks to strengthen such ecosystems by promoting collaboration among public institutions, private sector players and development partners. “One of the biggest challenges facing MSMEs is regulation, including policies governing how they operate, access to finance and business development services. The main objective of IYBA is creating decent employment opportunities for women and youth,” she said. According to Ndirangu, discussions during the forums demonstrated that no single institution can adequately address the challenges facing MSMEs. She said stronger partnerships among national and county governments, financial institutions, universities, innovation hubs and the private sector are essential in creating a business environment that enables enterprises to grow. She urged entrepreneurs to take advantage of public participation opportunities, noting that the policies currently under development will shape the future of the MSME sector. Ndirangu also commended MSEA, the Uasin Gishu County Government and other partners for supporting the initiative, reaffirming SNV’s commitment to strengthening entrepreneurship ecosystems that generate employment, particularly for women and young people. Senior Assistant Director at MSEA, Tabitha Gicheru, said the partnership with SNV had enabled many entrepreneurs, including youth, women and persons with disabilities, to better understand the policy and institutional framework supporting MSMEs. She welcomed the recently launched Revised MSME Policy 2026, saying it addresses long-standing challenges affecting the sector, including financing gaps, fragmented policy implementation and weak coordination among support institutions. Gicheru said the forums extended beyond raising awareness of policy reforms by bringing together key players within the entrepreneurship ecosystem to connect MSMEs with services and opportunities available in both the public and private sectors. “In the IYBA SEED Project, it is not just about the policy. We are bringing together entrepreneurship ecosystem players to demonstrate what government and the private sector can offer MSMEs so that this critical sector continues driving economic growth,” she said. MSEA North Rift Regional Director CPA Alton Kogo said the successful implementation of the programme in Uasin Gishu, Kisumu and Nakuru counties had paved the way for its expansion to other parts of the country. He said the final forum in Uasin Gishu brought together more than 60 entrepreneurs from sectors including textiles, welding, manufacturing, services, creative industries and beneficiaries of the National Youth Opportunities Towards Advancement (NYOTA) programme. Kogo said participants were introduced to a range of programmes and financial products offered by government institutions, commercial banks, innovation hubs and other ecosystem partners to help strengthen and grow their businesses. He noted that MSMEs remain a cornerstone of Kenya’s economy through their contribution to employment creation, innovation and wealth generation. “Many MSMEs are unaware of the programmes available within government institutions and the private sector. That is why we bring together ecosystem players such as banks, Kenya Bureau of Standards (KEBS), Kenya Revenue Authority (KRA), innovation hubs like EldoHub and Tumaini Hub, and the University of Eldoret to connect entrepreneurs with available opportunities,” he said. Kogo revealed that more than 500 youth and women entrepreneurs had been trained through the six policy awareness forums conducted across Uasin Gishu County. He expressed confidence that the programme would continue expanding to benefit more entrepreneurs nationwide while strengthening collaboration among institutions supporting enterprise development. The awareness campaign forms part of broader efforts to improve the policy and business environment for MSMEs by enhancing stakeholder participation, strengthening enterprise support systems and promoting access to financing, innovation and business development services. Organisers said the forums have laid the groundwork for stronger collaboration between entrepreneurs and policymakers while equipping MSMEs with the knowledge needed to access available opportunities and contribute more effectively to Kenya’s economic growth. Home»Agricultural Products»Kieni Crowns Sale 30 as NCE Moves 412 Tonnes of Coffee Worth Sh345 Million By Henry Kinyua The Nairobi Coffee Exchange auction of Wednesday, July 8, 2026, sold 6,622 bags weighing 412,562 kilogrammes (412.6 tonnes) for USD 2,669,895.60, about KES 345 million at KES 129.2 to the dollar. The market average was USD 324 per 50-kg bag, translating to about USD 6.47 (KES 836) per kilogramme of clean coffee. At the standard 6.5 kg of cherry to 1 kg of clean coffee, that implies a gross return of roughly KES 129 per kilogramme of cherry before deductions. Compared with Sale 29, the last auction before the recess, which traded 792,877 kg for USD 4,094,202 at an average of USD 258 per bag, Sale 30’s volume fell 48% and value 35%, but the average price jumped USD 65.58 (25%), lifting the implied cherry return from about KES 103 to KES 129 per kilogram. A thinner post-recess catalogue saw buyers chase the remaining quality lots harder. Top Prices The best price was USD 428 per 50-kg bag for Kieni Factory (Nyeri County, AA-RA/CAFÉ/EUDR-certified), sold through Alliance Berries to C. Dormans. Kathiguni (37KF0023) followed at USD 426, Kiiriangoro factory from Murang’a County at USD 425, Muthaite factory (38KF0008) and a second Kathiguni factory tied at USD 423, and Gitugu factory at USD 421. All were AA lots, and C.Dormans bought every one. Grade AA averaged USD 383; AB averaged USD 379 with a top of USD 420. Broker Performance Nine brokers traded. New KPCU PLC led in volume, with 136,795 kg (2,219 bags) worth USD 910,161 at an average price of USD 332.67. Alliance Berries followed with 86,514 kg for USD 604,270 at USD 349.23, then Kipkelion Broker Co. with 59,261 kg for USD 386,386 at USD 326.00, and KCCE Marketing Agency with 45,972 kg for USD 233,555 at USD 254.02. Coffee Estates Bourgeoisie Brokers posted the sale’s best average of USD 376 on 37,246 kg worth USD 280,018, driven by certified Maakiou estate lots. Minnesota Coffee Marketers sold 16,492 kg for USD 93,872 at USD 285; The new comers Ahadi Coffee 14,223 kg for USD 64,030 at USD 225.09 (weighed down by UG1 bulk); Kirinyaga Slopes 9,235 kg for USD 53,910 at USD 292; and Murang’a County Coffee Dealers 6,824 kg for USD 43,694 at a strong USD 320.15. Buyers Seventeen dealers bought coffee; the top five took over 84% of the volume. Ibero Kenya led tonnage with 96,917 kg (1,569 bags) for USD 680,930. C.Dormans bought 90,297 kg (1,478 bags) but wrote the biggest cheque, USD 714,073, reflecting its sweep of the premium lots. Louis Dreyfus took 87,958 kg (1,394 bags) for USD 545,431. How Murang’a County Is Using Technology, Market Access and Ecological Planning to Transform Agriculture. Home»Counties»How Murang’a County Is Using Technology, Market Access and Ecological Planning to Transform Agriculture By Kimuri Mwangi Murang’a County is implementing a targeted agricultural strategy that aligns farming interventions with its three ecological zones while leveraging technology, market linkages and value addition to increase productivity and improve farmers’ incomes. Speaking during the Financing Agri-Food Systems Sustainably (FINAS 2026) Conference in Nairobi, Governor Irungu Kang’ata said the county has designed programmes that respond to the unique agricultural potential of each region. “Murang’a is divided into three ecological zones. The upper region near the forest is mainly tea, the middle belt is coffee, and the lower region is dry,” he said. In the lower, drier parts of the county, Murang’a has prioritised commercial mango farming by linking growers with processors and exporters. The county facilitated contracts with juice manufacturers and organised farmers into cooperatives to strengthen their bargaining power. According to Kang’ata, the initiative has significantly improved returns to farmers, with mango prices rising from about KSh3 to KSh23 per kilogram. Companies purchasing mangoes include Sunday Mango and Premier Foods, some of which supply international beverage companies. “We are happy to be part of an international global value chain,” he said. The governor said the county’s role has been to correct market failures by connecting farmers to reliable buyers rather than directly subsidising production. For tea, Murang’a is working with Equity Bank to secure Geographical Indication (GI) status, an intellectual property system that protects products associated with a specific region and enables them to command premium prices. Kang’ata cited internationally recognised examples such as Champagne from France and Basmati rice, saying Murang’a tea could similarly gain added value through legal protection. He urged Parliament to fast-track legislation supporting geographical indications to accelerate the process. In the county’s coffee-growing belt, Murang’a is supplying agricultural lime to reduce soil acidity and improve nutrient uptake, helping farmers increase productivity at a time when coffee prices are improving. “The county has been giving farmers lime to reduce soil acidity and therefore help the coffee plant uptake nutrients,” Kang’ata said. Maize is grown across all three ecological zones, prompting the county to invest in measures aimed at increasing yields. These include distributing certified seed, supporting fertiliser use and assisting farmers with pest management. Kang’ata said many farmers continue to rely on recycled seed and insufficient farm inputs, limiting production. He argued that improving productivity remains central to reducing poverty. “Eradicating poverty in Kenya must start with making agriculture more productive,” he said. Murang’a has also digitised the distribution of agricultural support through itsInua Mkulimaprogramme. Using an electronic wallet system, eligible farmers receive support that can be redeemed at accredited agrovet and veterinary outlets for approved livestock and farm inputs. According to Kang’ata, the system has reduced theft, eliminated leakages associated with manual distribution and ensured support reaches intended beneficiaries. Automation has also strengthened county revenue collection, with Murang’a’s own-source revenue increasing from about Sh500 million to Sh1.7 billion over the past three years. The county is encouraging better post-harvest handling after grain buyers identified poor storage, early harvesting and inadequate drying as the main causes of aflatoxin contamination in maize. Rather than investing in grain dryers, Kang’ata said the county is focusing on farmer education to improve harvesting and storage practices. Kang’ata called for wider adoption of affordable farm machinery, including hand-held planters, to improve efficiency and lower production costs. He also stressed the need to connect farmers to dependable markets, noting that many grain processors continue importing maize from neighbouring countries because local production remains insufficient. “We have planted. How much are we going to sell?” he posed. Murang’a’s agricultural strategy combines ecological planning with market-oriented production. The county is promoting commercial mango farming through structured markets, pursuing premium branding for tea, improving coffee productivity through soil management, expanding maize production with quality inputs, digitising agricultural support and strengthening market access. Together, these interventions are intended to increase farm productivity, reduce inefficiencies and position agriculture as the county’s main driver of economic growth. Kirinyaga Leaders Protest Duty-Free Rice Imports as Government Cites Food Security. Home»Agriculture Politics»Kirinyaga Leaders Protest Duty-Free Rice Imports as Government Cites Food Security By Kimuri Mwangi A government decision to authorize the duty-free importation of 500,000 metric tonnes of white milled rice has drawn sharp criticism from Kirinyaga leaders, who argue the move undermines local farmers struggling with unsold stock. In a statement, Kirinyaga Senator Kamau Murango described the Gazette Notice dated 28th July 2025 as “a direct betrayal” of local rice farmers. “Instead of protecting and empowering local farmers, the government appears more committed to promoting collaborators who are rice importers, effectively rendering Kirinyaga rice farmers helpless in their own country,” Murango said. The Senator added that he had written to the Ministry of Agriculture and the National Treasury a month earlier seeking budgetary allocations to clear unsold rice stocks at the Mwea Irrigation Scheme. Former Kirinyaga County MP who is also the current Kenya Seeds Company Board Chairperson, Purity Wangui Ngirici, also expressed concern over the move. She noted that the government had recently assured over 8,500 Mwea farmers that the Kenya National Trading Corporation (KNTC) would purchase their unsold stock to make way for the upcoming harvest. “Before these rice imports begin flowing in, the government must fast-track its commitment to clear existing local rice stock,” Ngirici said. She added that farmers’ concerns over the rebranding of imported rice as Mwea rice had already undermined their market position. “To all concerned parties, this is not a protest, but a patriotic call to act in harmony with our policy commitments and support our dear farmers,” she said. However, the Agriculture and Food Authority (AFA) defended the decision, citing a severe production deficit. According to AFA Director General Dr. Bruno Linyiru, Kenya consumes approximately 1.3 million metric tonnes of rice annually, yet local production stands at only 264,000 metric tonnes, about 20% of national demand. “The approval for the duty-free importation is a timely and strategic intervention to address the current supply shortfall,” Linyiru said. He warned that failure to import rice would lead to food scarcity and significant price increases, affecting not only rice but also other staples such as maize and wheat products. Linyiru emphasized that the duty-free window, which will close on 31st December 2025, is a “targeted, short-term response” designed to stabilize prices and ensure food availability for low-income households. He added that the Kenya National Trading Corporation continues to procure rice directly from farmers and will provide a guaranteed market throughout the milling process. To safeguard quality, AFA said only Grade 1 milled white rice meeting Kenyan and international standards would be permitted, and all consignments must carry a Kenya Bureau of Standards (KEBS) Certificate of Conformity. Four Companies Dominate Animal Feed Market in Kenya Raising Food Prices and Hurting Farmers, says report. Home»Agriculture Politics»Four Companies Dominate Animal Feed Market in Kenya Raising Food Prices and Hurting Farmers, says report By Kimuri Mwangi There have been concerns regarding the quality and high animal feed prices in Kenya, which undermine livestock producers and lead to high food prices. This has seen some farmers exit dairy and poultry farming. Animal feed companies give all manner of excuses, from high government taxes to unavailability of locally grown raw materials. However, competition concerns have been raised as the animal feed market in Kenya appears to be concentrated, with a small group of producers accounting for the majority of the supply, alongside numerous small feed producers. Kenya’s livestock industry is under pressure due to rising animal feed prices driven by concentrated market structures, high input costs, and limited competition in both local and regional markets, according to a new report by the Competition Authority of Kenya (CAK). TheAnimal Feed Market Inquiry Report, 2024, highlights serious concerns about market dynamics that have led to sustained high feed prices, posing significant threats to livestock production, food affordability, and economic growth. The Inquiry reveals that Kenya’s commercial feed market is highly concentrated, with a few dominant players controlling much of the supply. Specifically, the largest four feed suppliers account for over 50% of commercial feed sales nationally. In poultry and dairy feed segments, the top four producers dominate around 75% of supply. Such market concentration, especially in oligopolistic conditions with relatively homogenous products, raises the risk of anti-competitive practices, including coordinated pricing, market division, and margin squeezing. An oligopolistic market is one dominated by a small number of firms, allowing them to exert significant influence over prices and output. This market structure is characterized by high barriers to entry, interdependence between firms, and potential for both competition and cooperation. Homogeneous products, in economics, refer to goods that are identical and indistinguishable from each other, regardless of the seller. These products are considered perfect substitutes, meaning consumers don’t have a preference for one over another based on any specific characteristics. Examples include commodities like cement, steel, and basic agricultural products like animal feeds. Several small feed producers in Kenya exited the market in 2022, primarily due to unaffordable input costs. These exits, according to the CAK, weaken competitive pressure and reduce consumer choice, further enabling dominant firms to maintain elevated prices. Feed producers in Kenya depend heavily on imported raw materials. Maize and its by-products are sourced locally, but key protein inputs like soybean meal and sunflower cake are largely imported from Zambia, Malawi, Uganda, and Tanzania. These input markets are also highly concentrated. The report notes that four regional company groupings dominate soy and sunflower processing across East and Southern Africa, allowing them significant control over pricing. “Despite inputs to animal feed being abundantly available in the region, feed prices have been extremely high,” the Inquiry observes. “Feed prices have been much higher than would be expected in well-functioning markets with effective competition.” Additionally, the report uncovers evidence of differential pricing where Kenyan feed producers pay more for inputs compared to buyers in countries like South Africa. This price discrimination, coupled with opaque broker-driven supply chains, raises suspicions of coordinated conduct by regional suppliers. High feed costs directly affect the cost of livestock products. Poultry, eggs, milk, fish, and beef prices rise as producers pass on the burden of expensive feed to consumers. For instance, prices of poultry feeds like broiler finisher pellets and layer mash surged by over 30% between mid-2021 and mid-2022, driven primarily by increased input costs. CAK’s analysis suggests that more competitive regional input markets could have saved Kenyan poultry feed producers over KShs. 3 billion annually. These savings could have translated into lower food prices, greater employment, and a more vibrant livestock sector. The report identifies structural and strategic barriers that hinder entry and growth of smaller feed manufacturers. These include difficulty accessing low-cost inputs, limited market reach due to lack of distribution networks and county-level taxes that fragment the domestic market and increase transaction costs. Some big animal feed companies also own or control the businesses that supply raw materials or breeding animals. This gives them more power and makes it easier for them to set prices in ways that might be unfair to smaller competitors. To restore competition and promote growth, the CAK recommends the following The CAK’s findings signal a wake-up call for policymakers, regulators, and industry players. With growing demand for affordable animal protein in Kenya, the sustainability and competitiveness of the animal feed sector are crucial. Without urgent reforms, the cycle of high feed costs and rising food prices may continue to erode the resilience of Kenya’s agricultural economy. “Ensuring regional markets are working well is essential for growth and job creation in animal feed and livestock production in Kenya and for competitive food prices,” the report opines. Tanzania,Malawi Trade to Resume Under New Bilateral Agreement. Home»Agriculture Politics»Tanzania,Malawi Trade to Resume Under New Bilateral Agreement Diplomatic negotiations between Tanzania and Malawi successfully culminated in an agreement to resume trade starting May 2, 2025. The high-level talks were led by Hon. Ambassador Mahmoud Thabit Kombo (MP), Tanzania’s Minister for Foreign Affairs and East African Cooperation, and Hon. Nancy Gladys Tembo (MP), Malawi’s Minister of Foreign Affairs, on May 2, 2025, in Dodoma. The discussions also involved Tanzania’s Minister of Agriculture, Hon. Hussein Bashe (MP), and Hon. Dr. Selemani Jafo (MP), Minister for Industry and Trade. Technical teams were headed by Permanent Secretaries from Tanzania’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs and East African Cooperation and the Ministry of Industry and Trade. Representing Tanzania, the Permanent Secretaries included Ambassador Dr. Samwel Shelukindo (Ministry of Foreign Affairs and East African Cooperation), Mr. Gerald Mweli (Ministry of Agriculture), and Dr. Hashil T. Abdallah (Ministry of Industry and Trade). A Joint Communiqué was signed on May 2, 2025, following the ministerial meeting involving the Foreign Affairs, Agriculture, and Industry and Trade ministries from both countries, held in Dodoma. The signing of the Joint Communiqué marks a reaffirmation of strengthened commercial ties between Tanzania and Malawi, particularly in the trade of agricultural products, in accordance with existing regional and international agreements and protocols. Tanzania warns Malawi and South Africa as countries ban each others agricultural produce.. Home»Agriculture Politics»Tanzania warns Malawi and South Africa as countries ban each others agricultural produce. A trade row has erupted between Tanzania, Malawi and South Africa following a ban imposed on the importation of various agricultural products originating from Tanzania. These products include flour, rice, ginger, bananas, and most recently, maize to Malawi and bananas to South Africa. Tanzania has not taken the move kindly, as the Agriculture Minister, Hussein Bashe, says this unilateral move has adversely affected Tanzanian traders who rely on the Malawian market for their produce.“It is important to note that for the past five years, the Government of Tanzania has made continuous efforts to secure market access for our bananas in South Africa, efforts which, unfortunately, have yet to yield results. This mirrors the long struggle we faced over the past decade in opening up the avocado market, which only saw progress when Tanzania began to impose reciprocal restrictions,” said Bashe in a statement.He added that there have been extensive but unsuccessful diplomatic engagements, which have necessitated the Ministry of Agriculture to reach retaliatory decisions. “If Malawi and South Africa do not reverse their decisions by Wednesday next week, the Government of Tanzania will impose a ban on the importation of all agricultural produce and agro-based products from these two countries. Transit of agricultural goods from Malawi and South Africa through Tanzanian territory, including through the Port of Dar es Salaam, will be suspended until further notice,” opined Minister Bashe. Following the decision, the export of fertilizer from Tanzania to Malawi will also be suspended, as part of measures to safeguard Tanzania’s national agricultural interests.In addition, all Tanzanian transporters involved in cross-border agricultural trade have been strongly advised to suspend any planned shipments to Malawi or South Africa until their respective governments review their positions.Furthermore, Tanzanian traders who have placed orders for apples, oranges, and other agricultural imports from South Africa have been advised to halt their transactions, as these goods will not be permitted entry into the Tanzanian market until South Africa opens its market to Tanzanian bananas.“As the Minister responsible, I wish to affirm that all efforts to engage Malawi’s Minister of Agriculture have been made, but unfortunately, no official response has been received. These measures are being undertaken in defence of our farmers, our traders, and the principle of fair regional trade. Tanzania will not continue to allow unequal market access to persist at the expense of its people,” added Minister Bashe. The countries’ trade war raises critical questions about the implementation of the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) agreement, which aims to create a seamless continental market. The trade restrictions, implemented by the countries, directly contradict AfCFTA’s core principles of reducing trade barriers and promoting intra-African commerce. Out of the 55 African Union member states, 54 have signed the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) agreement. Eritrea is the only member state that has not signed the agreement. Landmark Decision on Safeguard Measures by the COMESA Court of Justice. Home»Agriculture Politics»Landmark Decision on Safeguard Measures by the COMESA Court of Justice By Kimuri Mwangi While safeguards remain an important trade remedy measure for protecting domestic industries, countries should be wary of using them arbitrarily sometimes disguised as trade barriers. This is demonstrated in a landmark ruling by the First Instance Division (FID) of the COMESA Court of Justice (CCJ) in the case ofAgiliss Ltd v The Republic of Mauritiusand 4 others (Reference No.1 of 2019). The case involved the use of safeguard measures as a trade remedy within the Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA). Agiliss Ltd is a Mauritian company that imports basic commodities, including pre-packaged edible oils, from Egypt, another COMESA Member State. In 2018, the Government of Mauritius, citing the surge of imports in the domestic edible oil industry, invoked Article 61 of the COMESA Treaty (the Treaty) on safeguard measures and notified the COMESA Secretariat of its decision to impose a 10% customs duty on imported edible oils from the COMESA Member States. The safeguard measure was intended to protect the domestic industry. Aggrieved by this decision which had not been communicated, Agiliss Ltd engaged the Government in discussions but to no avail. Consequently, Agiliss Ltd filed the Reference before the COMESA Court of Justice, challenging the legality of the safeguard measure and seeking an order to prohibit the Government of Mauritius from imposing any customs duty or other non-tariff barriers on the import of edible oil into the Republic of Mauritius from the COMESA region. This case had been remitted back to the FID by the Appellate Division (AD) of the COMESA Court of Justice for the hearing of the main Reference. The AD overturned the decision earlier rendered by the FID declaring that it lacked jurisdiction because Agiliss Ltd had not exhausted local remedies under Article 26 of the Treaty. The AD held that there were exceptional circumstances in Mauritius which relieved Agiliss Ltd from the need to comply with the rule on exhaustion of local remedies. On 4thFebruary 2025, the FID delivered a landmark decision in favour of Agiliss Ltd. The Court found that the Government of Mauritius had violated the Treaty and the COMESA Regulations on Trade Remedy Measures, 2002 (the Regulations) in several key aspects. This included the failure to conduct a proper investigation, lack of stakeholder consultation, and the breach of notification obligations under the Treaty and the Regulations. The Court emphasised that the notification of the safeguard measure should have been sent to the COMESA Secretary General and all Member States, and not just the Secretary-General. The Court further highlighted that the process of imposing safeguard measures must comply with the Treaty and the Regulations from the very beginning. The Court declared that the decision of the Government of Mauritius to impose the safeguard measure and all consequential steps taken thereafter were null and void. The Court further restrained the Government of Mauritius from implementing the safeguard measure and ordered it to bear half of the costs of the Reference. This case sets a precedent for future trade disputes within COMESA and highlights the Court’s crucial role in maintaining the rule of law and interpreting the Treaty and its subsidiary legislation. Waithera replaces Wairimu as Improved Beans Expected to Raise Farm Incomes and Reduce Imports. Home»Science and technology»Waithera replaces Wairimu as Improved Beans Expected to Raise Farm Incomes and Reduce Imports By Kimuri Mwangi Beans have long been at the heart of Kenya’s food system, serving as the country’s second most important staple crop after maize and forming part of millions of households’ daily meals. Now, a new generation of improved bean varieties developed by researchers is expected to transform production by delivering higher yields, faster maturity, shorter cooking times and greater resilience to climate change, while helping Kenya reduce its dependence on imported beans. Developed through a long-standing partnership between the Kenya Agricultural and Livestock Research Organization (KALRO) and the Pan-Africa Bean Research Alliance (PABRA) programme of the Alliance of Bioversity International and CIAT, the newly released Waithera bean variety is being promoted as an innovation capable of improving food security, increasing farmers’ incomes and supporting sustainable agriculture. Jean Claude Rubiogo, the Global Bean Programme Leader at the Alliance of Bioversity International and CIAT and Director of PABRA, said beans remain one of Africa’s most important crops, occupying about 8.5 million hectares across the continent and approximately 1.3 million hectares in Kenya. “It’s a food security crop, second after maize. Everywhere you find maize and beans go together, not only in the farms, but also in our system, even in our food like githeri and others as well. People have rice and beans, chapati and beans, so it’s an important element of our food system,” Rubiogo described their significance. Researchers say the role of beans has evolved alongside changing climatic conditions and consumer preferences. Whereas older varieties took longer to mature and required several hours to cook, breeding efforts are now focused on varieties that mature in about 65 to 70 days, are ready for harvest in about 75 days and withstand diseases, pests and changing weather conditions. Consumer needs have also shaped the breeding programme. The improved varieties cook in nearly half the time required by older beans, have improved taste and have been developed to address digestive concerns associated with some traditional varieties. At the same time, they have been bred to meet market preferences, giving farmers a better opportunity to earn higher returns. One of Kenya’s best-known traditional bean varieties is Wairimu, released in 1982. While it served farmers for decades, researchers say it is characterised by lower yields, longer cooking time and lower consumer appeal. Its replacement, Waithera, has been developed to address those shortcomings. According to the researchers, the variety produces substantially more pods and seeds per plant, reaches maturity faster and offers significantly higher yields. Following its release last year, KALRO has licensed three seed companies to commercialise the variety, with additional companies expected to join the programme. KALRO Bean Seed Systems Researcher Dr David Karanja said the organisation is currently promoting both Nyota and Waithera, with the latter suited to areas where Nyota experiences challenges, particularly regions receiving higher rainfall. He said Waithera combines high productivity with resilience to climatic shocks and offers improved cooking qualities. “Waithera does not have flatulence issues. It’s high-yielding, and people are saying it has good soup, especially the women.” The variety is also finding new commercial opportunities. Besides serving institutional markets such as schools, a private sector partner has developed“Githeri on the Go,”a vacuum-packed, ready-to-eat product that requires no refrigeration and contains no additives, demonstrating the growing potential for value addition. The introduction of improved varieties comes as Kenya continues to face a production deficit. “Kenya currently produces about 650,000 metric tonnes of beans. And with that, we consume about 750,000 metric tonnes,” Karanja said. The annual shortfall forces the country to import between 150,000 and 250,000 metric tonnes of beans. Researchers believe wider adoption of improved varieties could substantially narrow that gap. Higher yields would enable farmers to harvest more from the same acreage while improving profitability. Karanja said that if about 40 per cent of Kenya’s bean acreage shifted to the new varieties, the country could significantly increase production and greatly reduce imports. Beyond boosting production, beans continue to play an important role in maintaining healthy soils. As legumes, they naturally fix nitrogen, reducing reliance on mineral fertilisers while improving soil fertility. Researchers say this makes them central to regenerative agriculture, particularly when grown in rotation or intercropped with maize, practices that help restore soil health and break disease cycles. Rubiogo noted that sustainable soil management cannot rely on chemical fertilisers alone and encouraged farmers to reintroduce legumes into their cropping systems to improve long-term productivity. Researchers also stress that improved seed alone is not enough. Successful adoption will depend on farmers embracing better production practices and working collectively to supply commercial markets. “Behavioural change is required for the farming community. Behavioural change on the varieties they use and how they use the varieties,” Karanja said. He added that farmers practising regenerative agriculture alongside good agricultural practices are achieving significantly higher yields. KALRO is therefore encouraging growers to form producer groups capable of supplying buyers with commercial volumes while taking advantage of improved varieties that require relatively few chemical inputs. With improved seeds now entering the market through licensed seed companies and KALRO Seeds, researchers believe Kenya is entering a new chapter in bean production, one that combines scientific innovation with climate resilience, sustainable farming and stronger food security while positioning the country to progressively reduce its reliance on imported beans. From Laboratory to Farm: KALRO Conference Pushes for Research That Changes Lives. Home»Science and technology»From Laboratory to Farm: KALRO Conference Pushes for Research That Changes Lives By Kimuri Mwangi Agricultural research must move beyond laboratories and deliver measurable improvements in farmers’ livelihoods if Kenya is to achieve food security, climate resilience and sustainable economic growth. That was the central message emerging from the 2nd Kenya Agricultural and Livestock Research Organization (KALRO) Scientific Conference and Innovation Expo 2026, where stakeholders outlined a roadmap for strengthening agricultural innovation and accelerating the adoption of research-driven technologies. The conference, held from 15 to 19 June 2026, brought together scientists, policymakers, development partners, private sector leaders, farmers, youth, academia and international institutions to deliberate on the future of agricultural research and transformation in Kenya and the wider region. Participants recognized agriculture as the backbone of Kenya’s economy, serving as a key source of employment, food security and export earnings. However, they noted that the sector continues to face mounting challenges, including climate change, declining soil health, emerging pests and diseases, food safety risks and inadequate commercialization of research outputs. A key outcome of the conference was the call for agricultural research to be evaluated by its real-world impact rather than the number of scientific publications produced. Delegates agreed that the success of research should be measured through farmer adoption of technologies, increased productivity, improved food security and enhanced livelihoods. To achieve this, participants called for stronger links between research institutions and farming communities through improved extension services, digital advisory platforms and structured commercialization pathways capable of moving innovations from research stations to farms. Climate change emerged as one of the defining issues discussed during the conference, with delegates warning that increasingly frequent droughts, floods and emerging pests pose an existential threat to Kenya’s food systems. The conference called for accelerated investment in climate-smart agriculture through the development and dissemination of drought-tolerant crop varieties, resilient livestock systems, improved soil and water conservation practices and early warning technologies capable of helping farmers adapt to changing weather patterns. Participants emphasized that building resilience within agricultural systems will be essential to safeguarding national food security in the coming years. Delegates described agricultural research as a strategic national investment rather than a cost, urging both national and county governments to significantly increase funding for research institutions. The conference called for sustained public financing, long-term funding mechanisms, modernization of research infrastructure, development of scientific talent and stronger public-private partnerships to strengthen Kenya’s agricultural research ecosystem. Participants noted that predictable investment is necessary to accelerate innovation and enable researchers to respond effectively to emerging agricultural challenges. The conference also identified digital agriculture and emerging technologies as critical tools for improving productivity and expanding farmers’ access to information and markets. Participants called for wider deployment of artificial intelligence, precision agriculture, earth observation technologies, digital extension services and integrated agricultural data systems to improve extension delivery and support smallholder farmers. Delegates said digital solutions have the potential to enhance decision-making while increasing efficiency across agricultural value chains. Commercialization of research outputs featured prominently during the conference, with delegates emphasizing that Kenya cannot achieve sustainable agricultural growth by relying on exports of raw agricultural commodities. Participants called for stronger agro-processing industries, clearer commercialization pathways for research innovations, expanded agribusiness incubation programmes and improved access to agricultural finance. The conference stressed that converting scientific innovations into commercially viable products would generate employment, strengthen value chains and increase farmers’ incomes. Recognizing that no single institution can transform agriculture independently, the conference reaffirmed the importance of collaboration among government agencies, research institutions, universities, CGIAR centres, development partners, private sector organizations and farmer groups. Delegates agreed that coordinated partnerships will be necessary to scale agricultural technologies, strengthen extension systems and mobilize sustainable financial resources for research and innovation. Participants emphasized that developing innovative agricultural technologies alone is insufficient if farmers and other potential users remain unaware of their existence or benefits. The conference called for stronger communication and visibility strategies to accelerate the adoption of research outputs through sustained public awareness campaigns, farmer-centred messaging, community engagement, media partnerships, digital platforms and knowledge-sharing initiatives. Research institutions, extension service providers and development partners were urged to translate scientific findings into clear, accessible and locally relevant information that can help farmers, agribusinesses, policymakers and consumers adopt proven technologies and practices. Delegates also underscored the importance of showcasing research success stories and measurable impacts to build public trust, stimulate demand and expand the adoption of innovations. The conference identified youth and women as central drivers of agricultural transformation rather than passive beneficiaries. Participants called for targeted investment in skills development, agribusiness incubation, technology transfer, access to finance and leadership opportunities to enable young people and women to play a leading role in transforming the agricultural sector. Food safety emerged as another major priority, with delegates expressing grave concern over persistently high aflatoxin contamination levels in maize and other staple crops. The conference warned that aflatoxin poses significant risks to public health, child nutrition, livestock productivity and national food security. Participants affirmed that aflatoxin contamination is preventable and recognized Aflasafe KE01, developed through KALRO’s collaboration with partners, as a proven biological control technology capable of reducing contamination by more than 80 percent. The conference recommended scaling deployment of Aflasafe KE01 in aflatoxin hotspot areas, incorporating the technology into national agricultural subsidy and e-voucher programmes, conducting nationwide public awareness campaigns on aflatoxin prevention and post-harvest management, and strengthening mycotoxin surveillance systems alongside county extension services. The conference adopted a series of resolutions aimed at strengthening Kenya’s agricultural research and innovation landscape. Participants resolved to strengthen demand-driven and impact-oriented agricultural research, accelerate the development and scaling of climate-smart technologies, expand digital agriculture and precision farming solutions, improve commercialization and technology transfer mechanisms, and strengthen food safety interventions with particular emphasis on aflatoxin management. Additional resolutions focused on promoting value addition and agro-industrialization, improving access to agricultural finance and innovation funding, strengthening farmer advisory and extension services, enhancing communication and visibility of agricultural technologies, expanding investment in youth- and women-led enterprises, mobilizing sustainable financing for research and advocating enabling policy frameworks that support agricultural innovation and technology adoption. At the close of the conference, participants reaffirmed their collective commitment to advancing science, innovation and evidence-based policymaking as essential pillars for building sustainable agri-food systems, strengthening climate resilience and improving livelihoods. They committed to ensuring that agricultural innovations are translated into practical, scalable solutions that reach farmers, create employment and contribute to the realization of Kenya’s Bottom-Up Economic Transformation Agenda (BETA), Vision 2030, the African Union Agenda 2063 and the Sustainable Development Goals. Turning Climate Challenges into Opportunities Through Smart Water and Soil Management. Home»Science and technology»Turning Climate Challenges into Opportunities Through Smart Water and Soil Management By Kimuri Mwangi As climate change tightens its grip on Africa’s agricultural systems through recurring droughts, erratic rainfall, and growing pressure on water resources, researchers, policymakers, and farming communities are increasingly turning to climate-smart solutions to safeguard food production. Against this backdrop, stakeholders gathered in Nairobi this week to assess the impact of the Climate Smart WATer Management and Sustainable DEVelopment for Food and Agriculture in East Africa (WATDEV) Initiative, a four-year programme that has been testing practical approaches to improving water and soil management in some of the region’s most climate-vulnerable landscapes. The conference provided a platform to showcase lessons and successes from the initiative, which has been implemented in Kenya, Ethiopia, Sudan and Egypt, while highlighting how local knowledge and scientific research can be combined to strengthen agricultural resilience. Held on June 15thto 16th, the International Conference on Integrating Grassroots Knowledge and Research into Water and Soil Management in Africa brought together researchers, policymakers, development partners, farmer organisations and local communities to review lessons learned from the initiative and explore pathways for scaling up successful interventions. The conference comes at a time when African agriculture faces mounting pressure from climate change, including erratic rainfall, prolonged droughts, land degradation and growing competition for scarce water resources. Against this backdrop, WATDEV has sought to bridge scientific research with grassroots knowledge to identify practical solutions capable of improving agricultural productivity while safeguarding natural resources. Funded through the European Union’s Development Smart Innovation through Research in Agriculture (DeSIRA) Initiative, WATDEV is implemented by the Italian Agency for Development Cooperation (AICS), with the Mediterranean Agronomic Institute of Bari (CIHEAM Bari)serving as the lead scientific institution. The programme is implemented in partnership with the Association for Strengthening Agricultural Research in Eastern and Central Africa (ASARECA), the Kenya Agricultural and Livestock Research Organization (KALRO), Ethiopia’s Water and Land Resources Centre (WLRC), Sudan’s Water Research Centre (WRC) and Heliopolis University of Egypt. According to ASARECA Director General Dr Sylvester Dickson Baguma, the project was designed to identify, test and validate best management practices that can strengthen sustainable and resilient agri-food systems in areas particularly affected by water scarcity and land degradation. “We are here for a close-out project meeting for WaterDev,” said Dr Baguma. “This project has been going on for four years and was intended to look at best management practices for managing soil and water for sustainable and resilient agri-food systems.” He noted that extensive field testing across the four participating countries had enabled the project team to develop a range of proven interventions tailored to different environmental and socio-economic conditions. “We have been able to develop several management practices that have been proven and tested. From that, we came up with a toolbox from which, depending on context, different farmers can choose and apply,” he said. The toolbox contains a range of climate-smart practices that farmers can adopt based on local conditions, including water conservation measures, irrigation innovations, intercropping systems and soil management approaches designed to improve resilience in drought-prone regions. Dr Baguma said the Nairobi conference was intended to assess what had worked, identify challenges encountered during implementation and document lessons that could inform future phases of the programme. “What we want to get from this conference is to take stock of what has worked, why it has worked, what has failed and why it has failed. From there, we get lessons learned for us to move into the future.” The initiative has particularly focused on semi-arid and arid regions where rainfall is scarce, and climate variability poses a major threat to food production. According to Dr Baguma, demonstrations have played a critical role in encouraging the adoption of climate-smart practices by farmers, and they wanted to make sure that the different tools they have tested and found working can help farmers in improving resilience of their food systems. He cited examples from Kenya’s drylands where pastoral communities have successfully adopted irrigation technologies to establish forage production systems, helping them secure feed supplies during dry seasons while generating additional income. “We have seen cases where irrigation is helping pastoralists grow pastures, and they are even able to sell some of these forages for dry-season feeding,” he said. The ASARECA chief emphasized that successful adoption depends on demonstrating clear economic benefits to farming communities. “What makes economic sense to farmers, they will take on because they know they are going to make money.” A key feature of WATDEV has been its emphasis on integrating scientific research with local knowledge systems. The initiative promotes a bottom-up approach that actively involves farmers and local communities in identifying challenges, testing solutions and validating technologies. This approach aligns with the broader objective of strengthening resilience in agro ecosystems while ensuring that innovations are practical, affordable and adapted to local realities. The programme has combined field demonstrations, eco-agro-hydrological modelling, training, capacity building, stakeholder engagement and policy dialogue to support sustainable water and soil management. Conference participants noted that such integrated approaches are increasingly important as climate change intensifies pressure on Africa’s natural resources and food systems. Agriculture currently accounts for approximately 70 per cent of global freshwater withdrawals and around 30 per cent of global energy consumption through food production and supply chains. With food demand expected to rise significantly by 2050, sustainable management of water and soil resources is becoming a central development priority. Among the Kenyan sites participating in the initiative, Tana River County has emerged as a significant example of how climate-smart interventions can be adapted to local conditions. Speaking during the conference, Laura Dema, County Energy Officer in the Department of Water, Energy, Mining and Natural Resources, outlined how the county has implemented two key Best Management Practices (BMPs) under the project: agroforestry and strengthening Water Users Associations (WUAs). Tana River is classified as an Arid and Semi-Arid Land (ASAL) county, where livelihoods are heavily dependent on both crop farming and livestock production. Most settlements are concentrated along the Tana River, while pastoral communities occupy the hinterland and frequently migrate in search of pasture during prolonged drought periods. The county’s vulnerability to drought made it an ideal location for testing interventions aimed at improving water security and strengthening climate resilience. Under WATDEV, implementation focused on the Hola and Bura Irrigation Schemes, two of the county’s most important agricultural production areas. Dema explained that the selection of the two BMPs followed consultations with local stakeholders and farmers, who played a central role in determining the most suitable interventions for their communities. During stakeholder forums held in Hola, farmers helped identify locations where agroforestry activities would be established and contributed to decisions on how the interventions should be designed. Initially, farmers proposed planting trees as windbreakers within farming areas. However, concerns emerged that trees could provide habitat for birds that damage rice crops, prompting a modification of the original plan. Instead, project stakeholders adopted a more targeted approach involving agroforestry in homesteads and the establishment of fodder-producing areas around irrigation schemes. Fruit trees, including mangoes, papayas and coconuts, were selected for planting within homesteads, where they can generate additional household income while providing environmental benefits. The agroforestry intervention is expected to diversify income sources for farming households, reducing dependence on seasonal crop production alone. Beyond income generation, the trees are expected to help mitigate wind erosion, a significant challenge in the largely flat landscape of Tana River County. “Strong winds affect crops in the schemes and also cause soil erosion,” Dema explained. The establishment of fodder zones around irrigation schemes is also expected to reduce tensions between crop farmers and pastoralists by providing livestock feed resources during dry periods. The second intervention under WATDEV in Tana River focused on strengthening existing Water Users Associations, which play a critical role in managing irrigation water distribution. Rather than creating new institutions, the project concentrated on building the capacity of existing associations through training and technical support. Dema said the capacity-building programme has improved water allocation and overall management of irrigation resources within the schemes. By involving communities directly in water governance, the initiative has strengthened local ownership of water resources and increased awareness of the need for efficient use. Tana River’s agricultural sector faces significant water challenges due to recurrent droughts, limited freshwater availability and increasing pressure on irrigation systems. According to Dema, empowering Water Users Associations to manage resources more effectively is expected to contribute to improved water availability and long-term sustainability. She added that efficient water use is also closely linked to energy savings, given the substantial costs associated with pumping water for irrigation. “When you use water effectively, the cost of energy that you put into pumping water is going to pay back what you produce,” she said. The initiative has therefore created a direct connection between water conservation, energy efficiency and agricultural productivity. As WATDEV approaches its conclusion, stakeholders are increasingly focused on ensuring that successful practices continue beyond the project’s lifespan. The Nairobi conference provided an opportunity to document achievements, identify gaps and explore opportunities for scaling up interventions across the region. For Dr Baguma, the lessons emerging from WATDEV demonstrate that climate resilience can be strengthened through practical, community-driven approaches that combine scientific evidence with local experience. “We need to manage our water very well. We need to manage our land and soil very well so that we can have resilient and sustainable agri-food systems,” he said. He added that Africa possesses the resources needed to feed itself, provided that water and soil resources are managed effectively and climate-smart agricultural practices are widely adopted. Museveni Commissions Uganda’s First Anti-Tick Vaccine & Production Plant. Home»Science and technology»Museveni Commissions Uganda’s First Anti-Tick Vaccine & Production Plant President Yoweri Kaguta Museveni recently commissioned Uganda’s first locally developed anti-tick vaccine at the National Livestock Resources Research Institute (NaLIRRI) in Namulonge, Nansana Municipality, marking a major scientific breakthrough in the fight against tick-borne livestock diseases. The UShs 170 billion government-funded initiative is aimed at addressing one of the most persistent threats to Uganda’s livestock sector. Speaking during the launch of both the vaccine and its production facility, President Museveni described ticks as a long-standing challenge in key cattle-keeping regions such as Sheema, Kajara and parts of Kashari, where farmers continue to suffer major livestock losses despite the absence of tsetse flies. He said the vaccine offers a safer and more sustainable solution compared to conventional acaricides, which he noted are toxic and lose effectiveness over time. “The cow now kills the tick,” he said. President Museveni also reaffirmed his commitment to strengthening Uganda’s scientific sector, proposing tax incentives for scientists similar to those granted to security personnel in recognition of their contribution to national development. He congratulated the scientists behind the innovation and pledged continued government backing, including confirmation of the government’s commitment to provide the UShs600 billion requested by the National Agricultural Research Organisation (NARO) for the establishment of a vaccine research hub. Minister of Agriculture, Animal Industry and Fisheries, Hon. Frank Tumwebaze, hailed the achievement, noting that the project began in 2022 when scientists first presented the proposal. He commended both the President and the government for supporting the process that has now led to the successful development of the vaccine. State Minister for Animal Industry, Hon. Bright Rwamirama, praised sustained government investment in the livestock sector, highlighting significant growth in dairy farming and rising livestock populations. NARO Director General Dr. Yonah Baguma described the vaccine launch as both a scientific and economic milestone. He said the vaccine is expected to protect Uganda’s estimated 16 million cattle while significantly reducing dependence on imported vaccines and acaricides. According to Dr. Baguma, the production facility has the capacity to manufacture up to 36 million doses annually, with current production standing at approximately 3 million doses per month. He added that 1.5 million doses have already been produced, with demand emerging from neighbouring countries, with the initiative generating hundreds of jobs and positioning Uganda as a potential regional hub for veterinary vaccine production. The vaccine, branded NAROVAC-ATV1, is a recombinant product developed using antigens (Subolesin) derived from local tick species. It is administered to cattle from one month of age, with an initial three-dose schedule at day 0, day 30 and day 180, protecting for one year. Booster doses are required in subsequent years. National Drug Authority (NDA) Executive Director Dr. David Nahamya confirmed that the manufacturing plant meets required standards, noting that the authority provided technical oversight throughout the vaccine’s development. He added that farmers who participated in early trials have reported encouraging results, including reduced tick infestations and lower treatment costs. The vaccine is expected to retail at approximately UShs10,000, making it an affordable option for livestock farmers. The development is expected to significantly lower the cost of tick control, reduce dependence on acaricides and improve productivity in Uganda’s livestock industry.
